2.title: Long-Term Health & Care Insurance Products

subsections.title: Overview of Long-Term Health & Care Products
bullets: Health and care insurance products operate on the principle that a policyholder pays premiums now in exchange for a promise of future benefit — potentially many years later — upon the occurrence of a health-related event (illness, disability, death, or care need).

Three broad product categories: Protection-type — lump sum on a specified event (e.g., CI); Income-type — ongoing income replacement (e.g., IP); Cost-coverage type — meets specific costs incurred (e.g., private medical, long-term care).
Both insurer and policyholder have distinct goals: the insurer seeks adequate return for risk taken; the policyholder wants needs met at reasonable cost.

subsections.title: Benefits and Features
bullets: Also known as Permanent Health Insurance (PHI) or Disability Income Insurance (USA). Core purpose: replace a portion of lost earnings when the insured cannot work due to illness or accident (incapacity).
Long-term policy; multiple claim periods possible without the policy lapsing. Term is either fixed or runs to a specified age (typically retirement age).
Benefits are level or escalating (linked to earnings or prices index), in or out of claim. No benefit is paid if no claim arises.
Deferred (elimination) period: a waiting period at the start of incapacity before benefits commence. Typical lengths: 13, 26, or 52 weeks. Purpose: avoids short-term claims and reduces moral hazard.

Definitions of incapacity: Own occupation — inability to perform the insured's specific job (more generous; harder to assess objectively); ADL-based — inability to perform defined tasks; more objective.

Common exclusions: unemployment/redundancy/early retirement, alcohol/drug abuse, self-inflicted injury, attempted suicide, war, civil commotion, failure to follow medical advice, hazardous sports, criminal acts.

Preventing reluctance to return to work: benefit capped relative to salary (replacement ratio typically <100% net of tax), claims monitoring and counsellors, rehabilitation programmes, targeting occupations with strong work ethic.
Linked claims: a claim may restart without a new deferred period if incapacity recurs from the same cause within a defined period of returning to work.
Partial/proportionate benefits available if the insured returns to work part-time or in a less demanding role.
Rehabilitation benefit encourages and supports return to work, benefiting both insurer and insured.

Short-term alternatives: Accident & Sickness (A&S) policies — annually renewable, cover 2–5 years. More flexible but less certainty of cover; useful as affordable alternative or to fill deferred period gap.
Premiums are usually level but may be index-linked. May be reviewable (insurer can revise) or guaranteed. Guaranteed premiums are more expensive due to reserve requirements.

key_terms.definition: Income Protection Insurance — replaces lost earnings during incapacity due to illness or accident. Also known as PHI or Disability Income Insurance.
Waiting period at the start of incapacity before IP benefits commence; typical lengths 13, 26, or 52 weeks.
Incapacity defined as inability to perform the insured's specific job — more generous but harder to assess objectively.
IP benefit expressed as a percentage of pre-incapacity earnings; kept below 100% net of tax to preserve return-to-work incentive.
A recurring claim from the same cause within a defined period of returning to work that restarts without a new deferred period.
Premium the insurer can revise at periodic intervals, typically to reflect updated morbidity experience.
Accident & Sickness policy — short-term (annually renewable) alternative to full IP cover; covers 2–5 years.

exam_qa.q: Why is the IP benefit replacement ratio kept below 100% of net earnings?
exam_qa.a: To preserve the insured's financial incentive to return to work. If the benefit matched or exceeded net take-home pay, the policyholder would have no financial reason to end their claim and resume employment.

exam_qa.q: What is the purpose of a deferred period in IP insurance?
exam_qa.a: It avoids short-term claims (which are expensive to administer relative to benefit paid) and reduces moral hazard by ensuring only genuine long-term incapacity triggers a benefit. It also aligns with employer sick pay periods.

subsections.title: Insurer's Perspective
bullets: Occupation is a major rating factor — high-risk occupations may be declined or loaded. Medical and family history may restrict acceptance. Policy conditions must be unambiguous to avoid disputes.

Claims management: replacement ratio must preserve return-to-work incentive; regular medical certification and monitoring; increasing focus on rehabilitation; claim info checked against application data.
Dangerous to use standard morbidity tables without adjustment — experience varies widely by insurer.
External factors (recessions, unemployment trends) affect claims frequency and duration.
Raising premiums risks selective lapsing — healthier lives leave, worsening the remaining risk pool. Regulatory approval may be required before increases.
Group: 'free cover' available up to a threshold (no individual underwriting). Requires minimum scheme size, compulsory membership, and 'actively at work' condition.

key_terms.definition: Maximum benefit amount in a group IP scheme below which individual underwriting is not required.
Healthier policyholders lapsing after a premium increase, leaving a worse (higher-risk) remaining pool.

exam_qa.q: Why is IP insurance particularly difficult to price?
exam_qa.a: Morbidity experience varies widely by insurer, occupation, and economic environment. Standard tables need significant adjustment. External shocks (recessions) increase claim rates. Selective lapsing after premium increases can rapidly deteriorate the risk pool.

subsections.title: Meeting Customer Needs
bullets: Primary customer groups: (a) Employers — pass long-term sick pay liability to insurer after initial period; (b) Self-employed — no employer safety net; (c) Individuals without employer provision.

Needs addressed: income replacement (especially for those with dependants); loan/mortgage servicing; premium waiver (continues funding other policies such as pensions during incapacity); locum cover for small professional practices.
Where State disability benefits are low, IP fills a clear gap. Where State benefits are generous and complex, integrating products may add complexity without value.
State sick pay (e.g., UK statutory sick pay) typically covers only the early weeks of illness — IP bridges the long-term gap.

key_terms.definition: IP benefit variant that continues premium payments on other policies (e.g., pension) during incapacity.
IP variant for small professional practices funding a replacement professional during the insured's incapacity.

subsections.title: Benefits and Features
bullets: Also known as Dread Disease, Serious Illness, Crisis Cash, or Living Assurance. Core structure: lump sum payable upon diagnosis (or surgical procedure) of a specified condition.
Minimum conditions in the UK: cancer, heart attack, stroke (must be included in all CI policies).
Benefit triggers — three types: (1) occurrence of a defined event; (2) reaching a defined level of impairment; (3) undergoing a specified surgical procedure.

Policy variants: stand-alone CI (benefit only on CI event); accelerated CI (rider on a life policy — pays on CI or death, whichever first); tiered/partial benefit (proportion of sum insured for less severe conditions); simplified CI (fewer conditions, lower cost).
Additional covers often included: terminal illness benefit; Total Permanent Disability (TPD); children's benefit.

Survival period: most stand-alone policies require the insured to survive a set period (commonly 30 days) after diagnosis before benefit is paid — avoids paying on death when no CI pricing was assumed.
Waiting period: some policies exclude claims arising during an initial period after policy inception.

Not an indemnity product: CI does not replace a measurable financial loss — the lump sum is fixed regardless of actual costs. Windfall payments possible where actual hardship is minimal (e.g., routine angioplasty).
Premium basis: reviewable or guaranteed. Guaranteed premiums carry higher insurer risk given uncertainty in future CI incidence and medical advances.

key_terms.definition: Critical Illness Insurance — pays a lump sum on diagnosis of a specified condition. Not an indemnity product.
CI rider on a life policy that pays the sum insured on whichever occurs first — CI diagnosis or death.
Period (typically 30 days) the insured must survive after diagnosis before a CI benefit is paid.
Total Permanent Disability — lifestyle-threatening disability that is not necessarily life-threatening; often included alongside CI cover.
CI benefit paid where actual financial hardship is minimal (e.g., a fully-recovered routine procedure), creating a mismatch between benefit and need.
CI variant paying a proportion of the sum insured for less severe conditions while retaining full cover for more serious ones.

exam_qa.q: Why is CI insurance described as 'not an indemnity product'?
exam_qa.a: The lump sum is fixed at outset and paid regardless of actual financial loss suffered. Unlike IP (which replaces measurable lost income) or PMI (which reimburses actual treatment costs), CI pays a set amount that may be more or less than the policyholder's real need.

exam_qa.q: What is the purpose of the survival period in CI insurance?
exam_qa.a: To avoid paying a CI benefit where the insured dies very shortly after diagnosis — as CI policies are priced on the assumption of survival, not death. Without the survival period, a life policy with accelerated CI could effectively pay twice for the same event.

subsections.title: Insurer's Perspective
bullets: CI is especially vulnerable to anti-selection given the lump sum nature — people may be aware of elevated health risk at application. Underwriting and claims checks are key controls.
IBNR reserves needed — claims may be notified up to 10 years after the triggering event.

Definitional risk: medical advances may render covered conditions less severe or more treatable — the insurer may be forced to pay for less serious versions of named conditions than originally priced.

New diseases (e.g., AIDS): a positive-test trigger would create massive windfall payments; likely covered under TPD for severe/permanent cases only. Not expected to be added to CI policies due to pricing difficulty.
Group CI: anti-selection restricted via free cover limits, active-at-work requirements, exclusions, take-up rate monitoring, and take-over terms.

key_terms.definition: Incurred But Not Reported — reserve for claims that have occurred but not yet been notified to the insurer.
Risk that medical advances change what constitutes a covered condition, forcing the insurer to pay on less severe cases than priced.
Conditions under which an insurer takes over a group CI scheme from another insurer, including treatment of pre-existing claims.

exam_qa.q: How does medical advance create risk for CI insurers?
exam_qa.a: Conditions originally considered life-threatening (e.g., some cancers, heart attacks) become increasingly treatable. The insurer may be obliged to pay full CI benefits for conditions that no longer cause significant long-term impairment, increasing claim frequency without a corresponding increase in severity-adjusted pricing.

subsections.title: Meeting Customer Needs
bullets: The financial consequences of surviving a serious illness can exceed those of death: costs continue or increase while earnings fall. The earner's carer may also lose income.

Uses of the CI lump sum: fund medical treatment/surgery to speed recovery; repay mortgage or loans; convert to income stream (annuity) if unable to work; fund lifestyle change; fund home adaptations or specialist equipment; support a dependant; business protection (partner buyout); rehabilitation and convalescence; domestic help.
Key attraction to consumers: the lump sum is unconditional (not reclaimed on recovery), not means-tested, and gives complete flexibility over use.

Limitation: because CI is not an indemnity product, the sum insured may be too much (windfall) or too little (shortfall) for actual needs. Other products may be more targeted (IP for income, PMI for medical costs).
CI covers headline conditions that are perceived as serious and frequent, clearly definable, and priceable from available data — it is not a comprehensive illness policy.
Group CI is a valuable employee benefit, particularly for those for whom IP is unavailable (e.g., blue-collar workers). TPD inclusion adds further value.

exam_qa.q: Why might the financial impact of a serious illness exceed that of death?
exam_qa.a: On death, expenditure typically ceases along with income. On serious illness, the individual continues to incur living costs (and possibly additional care/treatment costs) while earnings may fall significantly or stop. A carer (often a partner) may also have to reduce or cease work, compounding the household income loss.

subsections.title: Benefits and Features
bullets: Purpose: finance the costs of personal and nursing care in old age, for individuals not expected to recover to independence. LTCI is not about curing illness but about supporting daily living and maintaining wellbeing.

Costs of care fall into three categories: living costs (food, clothing, heating); housing costs (rent, mortgage, council tax); personal care (bathing, dressing, nursing — costs arising from frailty/disability). LTCI is typically targeted at the additional personal care costs only.
Claim trigger for pre-funded LTCI: inability to perform a specified number of Activities of Daily Living (ADLs) — e.g., bathing, dressing, eating. Cognitive impairment may also trigger a claim.

Pre-funded LTCI: premiums paid in advance. Regular premiums (usually reviewable; may escalate) or single premium. Waiver of premium commonly included when a claim commences. Premiums typically cease at a specified age (e.g., 90) or when care commences.

Benefit types: single lump sum; annuity certain; lifetime benefit subject to ongoing disability; restricted benefit (capped period or total amount). Benefit amount may be fixed, index-linked, or indemnity (up to a monetary cap).
Deferred period applied to ensure only genuinely long-term care is covered.
Optional death benefit if death occurs before or shortly after a claim commences.
Benefit may be paid to policyholder, family member, or directly to the care provider.
Innovative pre-funded forms: rider on CI or IP policy; unit-linked investment bond; component of a pension product.

Immediate Needs LTCI: purchased at the point care is already required (single premium, often funded by sale of property). Provides a guaranteed regular income for life, protecting against longevity risk. Benefits may escalate. Optional death benefit if death occurs within a short period after inception.

key_terms.definition: Long-Term Care Insurance — finances personal and nursing care costs in old age for those not expected to recover to independence.
Defined everyday tasks (e.g., bathing, dressing, eating) used as objective claim triggers in IP and LTCI policies.
LTCI where premiums are paid in advance of care being needed; may be regular or single premium.
LTCI purchased at the point of care need (single premium); provides guaranteed income for life and protects against longevity risk.
LTCI feature whereby premiums cease when a claim commences, so the policyholder is not paying premiums while receiving benefits.

exam_qa.q: What is the difference between pre-funded LTCI and Immediate Needs LTCI?
exam_qa.a: Pre-funded LTCI is purchased in advance of care being needed, with premiums paid over time to build up cover. Immediate Needs LTCI is a single-premium product purchased at the point care is already required (often funded by selling a property), providing a guaranteed income for life to cover ongoing care costs.

subsections.title: Insurer's Perspective
bullets: Pre-funded LTCI risk: earlier-than-expected claims if insufficient premiums have been received before a claim commences. Managed via deferred period, comprehensive underwriting, maximum entry age (e.g., 60), and 'actively at work' requirement.
Longevity/improving life expectancy: longer claim durations than expected; higher claim inception rates (e.g., stroke survivors needing care).

Interest rate risk: index-linked or indemnity benefits require matching investment returns. Low interest rate environments make this difficult, potentially necessitating premium increases or benefit reductions. Alternative: negotiate fixed annual cost increase directly with care providers.

Claims management: ADL-based definitions can be subjective (e.g., 'adequate' washing standard); care requirements may fluctuate requiring regular review; fraud risk if benefits paid directly to care providers; insurer should maintain contact with policyholder or independent family member.
Underwriting: current activity levels give an indication of likely care timeline; cognitive testing may form part of underwriting; cognitive impairment trigger adds complexity.
Guarantees add to capital requirements; typically limited to defined periods (e.g., 5 or 10 years); may convert to guaranteed once policyholder reaches a given age (e.g., 70).
Unit-linked design: lower initial premiums with investment growth expected to fund future care costs. Reduces insurer's early risk but introduces investment risk for the policyholder.

key_terms.definition: Risk that policyholders live (and claim) longer than expected, increasing total claim costs.
Risk that the cost of care services rises faster than the investment returns backing index-linked or indemnity LTCI benefits.

exam_qa.q: Why does LTCI present particular interest rate risk for insurers?
exam_qa.a: Index-linked and indemnity LTCI benefits must be backed by investments generating sufficient real returns. In low interest rate environments, returns may not keep pace with care cost inflation, forcing the insurer to either raise reviewable premiums (risking lapse) or reduce benefits (not possible if guaranteed), potentially creating a solvency strain.

subsections.title: Meeting Customer Needs
bullets: Core need: fund care costs in old age without depleting assets or burdening family.
Informal care trend: geographic mobility means family members are less available to provide free care — increasing demand for formal (and thus insured) care.

Asset protection: long-term care costs can rapidly erode wealth including the family home. LTCI allows assets (and inheritance) to be preserved. Means-tested State benefits reduce personal income and dignity.

State provision interaction: where State offers no support, LTCI fills the entire gap; where State offers means-tested support, LTCI preserves assets above the means-test threshold and offers greater choice of care provider; unclear State/individual boundary suppresses demand.
UK example: proposed lifetime cap of £86,000 on individual care costs in England (from October 2025; excludes accommodation and food costs).
Catastrophic cost risk: those with no insurance and outside State provision face potentially unlimited care costs — the key driver of demand for LTCI.

key_terms.definition: State care support available only to those whose assets fall below a defined threshold — LTCI can preserve assets above this threshold.
UK policy proposal limiting individual liability for personal care costs to £86,000 (excluding accommodation); designed to reduce catastrophic cost risk.

exam_qa.q: Why might demand for LTCI be suppressed even where care costs are catastrophically high?
exam_qa.a: The unclear boundary between State and individual responsibility makes it difficult for individuals to assess their actual exposure, reducing their perceived need to insure. Additionally, people tend to underestimate longevity and the probability of requiring care, and the premiums for pre-funded LTCI can be high relative to take-home income.

2.summary:
Income protection insurance

Benefits and features
• Regular payments when unable to work through illness or accident after an initial deferred period.
• Unemployment, redundancy, early retirement, reluctance to work not covered. Benefit paid for fixed term or expiry age.
• Premiums level or inflation linked, reviewable based on portfolio claims experience or guaranteed.
• Benefits level or escalating in/out of claim.
• May include rehabilitation or proportional benefits or linked claims.

Insurer's perspective
• Underwriting and claims – complex underwriting, (especially for higher risk occupations and if concerns over past / family medical history). Exclusions limit claims and clear policy conditions help. ADLs give objective test of incapacity.
• Policy conditions – benefit limits need to give incentive to return to work and consider State benefits.
• Premiums – shouldn't use standard published experience without adjustment, guaranteed premiums are expensive, may not be able to increase reviewable premiums enough to cover costs.
• Group cover – free cover for group IP business reduces underwriting.
• Risk management – risk control important for IP insurance, includes underwriting/pricing by risk factors, claims management and monitoring claims.

Meeting customer needs
• Provides replacement income to meet monthly loan costs (eg mortgage payments).
• Small professional practices may use to cover temporary replacement of one partner.
• Greatest need from employers, self-employed and individuals with no employer provision.

Critical illness insurance

Benefits and features
• Lump sum benefit paid on one of a defined list of conditions/events.
• May be a minimum set of conditions required to be covered, or a simplified product (eg cancer only).
• Available with accelerated benefits on death.
• Premiums reviewable or guaranteed.

Insurer's perspective
• Underwriting and claims – avoid anti-selection and non-disclosure, careful policy wording to avoid excessive claims, use standardised CI definitions.
• Group cover – reduce anti-selection via exclusions, free cover limits, ‘actively at work’ requirements, minimum take-up rates on voluntary schemes, setting out take-over terms for taking over schemes.
• Premiums – guaranteed premiums may have significant cost, increases may be required, could add to cover to make more attractive (but consider whether real value to customers).
• Future – uncertain due to medical advances around severity of critical illnesses.

Meeting customer needs
• Amount of benefit – reduce financial consequences of CI diagnosis (including medical costs / care, loan repayments, provide an income, change lifestyle, make adaptations to home and rehabilitation). Business partners use to buyout partner. Cash lump sum benefits give choice. May be a windfall if benefit far outweighs costs.
• Diseases covered – not a comprehensive list, risk over confusion around cover.
• Group cover – used to attract and retain staff, may be useful where IP insurance is unavailable (eg certain high risk occupations).

Long-term care insurance

Benefits and features
• Insurance vehicle for financing long-term care costs.
• Pre-funded LTCI – regular / single premium paid in advance of claim; claims assessed against ADLs; may be a deferred period before claims are paid; benefits: lump sum, annuity, paid for lifetime or a restricted period; can be fixed, index-linked or indemnity; optional death benefit for early death after claim incepts.
• Immediate needs LTCI – paid by single premiums at / shortly after point that care is required; can be funded by property sale (unless partner / dependent living there); benefits can be paid for lifetime / fixed period, fixed amounts / increasing; optional death benefit for early death.

Insurer's perspective
• Pre-funded LTCI – risk from early claims before premiums cover costs of benefit, deferred periods help manage this.
• Claims – may be longer than expected due to improving life expectancy, particularly in ill-health. Care needed over index-linked or indemnity benefits, could cap increases via provider arrangement. Claim conditions need to be clearly stated to avoid ambiguity and claims managed in payment to ensure level of care appropriate.
• Underwriting – need sufficient information, may include cognitive testing.
• Guarantees – adds cost to the insurer, could restrict period offered for.
• Unit-linked design – reduces risk for the insurer and lowers policyholder premiums.

Meeting customer needs
• Where no / limited State provision – reduces the risk of high costs of care, can provide control over care provision. May help protect assets for children / grandchildren on death.
• Where some State provision – can be confusion around who is responsible for provision of long-term care, need clear boundaries to identify need for LTCI.

3.title: Short-term Health & Care Insurance Products

subsections.title: Benefits and Features
bullets: PMI reimburses policyholders for medical treatment costs; level of cover depends on what the State provides.
In countries without State healthcare, PMI replaced the fee-for-service model and reduced individuals' financial risk. Cover may or may not be compulsory.
Where a comprehensive State system (e.g. NHS) exists, PMI provides choice over how, where, and when care is delivered — typically for acute/treatable conditions, not chronic or A&E.
PMI can be seen as complementary to (or competing with) State healthcare.

Typical exclusions: drug abuse, self-inflicted injuries, out-patient drugs/dressings, normal pregnancy, cosmetic surgery, gender reassignment, preventative treatment, dialysis, mobility aids, experimental treatments/drugs, organ treatment, war risks, hazardous hobbies.
Exclusions are more common when PMI supplements a State system; harder to enforce where no State system exists (though cosmetic/weight-loss may still be excluded).
Claims may be paid to the care provider directly, or reimbursed to the insured.
PMI is generally annually renewable. Premiums can be rated by age, gender, and prior medical history — subject to local regulation.
Where compulsory, rating factors may be restricted to prevent individuals becoming uninsurable (e.g. Switzerland: no age or medical condition rating permitted).

key_terms.definition: Private Medical Insurance — indemnity-based cover for medical treatment costs.
Pre-PMI model where individuals paid all medical costs out of pocket.
Dangerous hobbies typically excluded from PMI cover.
Policy reviewed and repriced each year.

exam_qa.q: In what ways does PMI compete with a State healthcare system?
exam_qa.a: Competes for the same pool of doctors, nurses, hospital beds, and diagnostic services. Private care uses the same professionals as the State system, so more time spent on private patients reduces availability for State patients.

subsections.title: Insurer's Perspective
bullets: Indemnity basis → risk of very large claims. Imposing policy limits controls this but breaks the true indemnity principle.
Rising claims costs can be managed via HMO/PPO arrangements, though this restricts policyholder choice.
HMO: network of care providers — cover restricted to within the network.
PPO: preferred list of providers — insurer has negotiated preferential rates, reducing costs for both parties.
High/unexpected claim rates managed by: excluding pre-existing conditions, underwriting, GP referrals as gatekeepers.
Excesses, coinsurance, and no-claims discounts reduce small claim frequency and drive down premiums.
ACA (USA): individual mandate ensured younger/healthier lives entered the risk pool; also capped annual out-of-pocket expenses.
Wellness incentives (activity tracking, dietary monitoring) can reduce claims.

key_terms.definition: Health Maintenance Organisation — care only provided within the network.
Preferred Provider Organisation — insurer-approved list with negotiated rates.
Higher-risk individuals being more likely to take out or retain cover.
US ACA requirement to hold health insurance, with a financial penalty for non-compliance.
Insured retains a fixed percentage of claim costs (e.g. insured pays 20%, insurer 80%).

subsections.title: Meeting Customer Needs
bullets: Where State cover exists, PMI enables faster access, choice of hospital/consultant, and better comfort (e.g. private room).
UK NHS focuses on chronic/life-threatening conditions; non-emergency procedures can involve very long waits — PMI addresses this.
6-week waiting list plans: PMI only activates if NHS wait exceeds 6 weeks — cheaper than comprehensive PMI.
Employers may offer PMI as a benefit to key staff to minimise business disruption from illness.

key_terms.definition: PMI that only covers treatment when NHS waiting time exceeds 6 weeks; lower premium than full PMI.

exam_qa.q: Why might someone pay to see a consultant privately if they could see the same person on the NHS?
exam_qa.a: To avoid long waiting lists (speed matters clinically and for peace of mind). May wish to bypass GP referral. More choice of consultant. Private consultants typically spend more time per patient and may offer higher perceived quality of diagnosis.

subsections.title: Benefits and Features
bullets: Annually renewable, defined benefit products — primarily UK market.
Benefits paid as cash directly to the policyholder (not reimbursement to providers).
Covered events: dental, optical, physiotherapy, maternity, hospitalisation, recuperation, hearing aids, consultations.
Premiums are community-rated and generally low (fixed benefit product).

key_terms.definition: Fixed cash payment per event — not linked to actual cost incurred.
Same premium charged to all policyholders regardless of individual risk factors.
Period after policy start during which no claims can be made.

subsections.title: Insurer's Perspective
bullets: Fixed benefits reduce risk of very large claims (unlike indemnity products).
More-than-expected claims managed by: waiting periods, pre-existing condition exclusions, no-claims discounts, treatment frequency limits.
Potential for over-recovery (claim exceeds actual cost) — mitigated by capping benefit or applying coinsurance.
Low premiums mean insurer depends heavily on renewals to recover acquisition costs.

key_terms.definition: When the defined benefit paid exceeds the actual cost incurred by the policyholder.

subsections.title: Meeting Customer Needs
bullets: Suitable where full PMI is unaffordable — provides access to specific treatments (dental, optical, physio) without full cost.
Not designed for major procedures or operations.
Complements PMI: PMI typically excludes dental and optical; a cash plan fills this gap.
Fixed hospitalisation cash payment can cover incidental costs: transport, childcare, etc.

subsections.title: Benefits and Features
bullets: USA-specific product (note: UK Glossary defines a different variant).
Comprehensive PMI-type product covering primary, secondary, and tertiary care — focus is on capping exposure to large costs, not full indemnity.
Introduced in the USA in the 1950s as medical costs rose significantly.
Coinsurance: insured retains a share of costs — key difference from PMI.
Deductible (excess): insured pays the first tranche of costs; insurer only pays above this.
Maximum benefit limits may apply per year, per lifetime, or per benefit category.

key_terms.definition: Policyholder retains a fixed % of all claim costs (e.g. 10% after deductible).
Amount the policyholder must pay before any insurance benefit is paid (also called excess).
Defined list of treatments/procedures that the policy will pay for.
General term for the portion of claim costs retained by the insured.
MME variant that also covers smaller costs, not just large/catastrophic expenses.

subsections.title: Insurer's Perspective
bullets: Not a true indemnity product — insured always retains a share above the deductible.
Very large claims still possible → policy limits and/or reinsurance may be appropriate.
Greater flexibility → more anti-selection and more complex pricing.

key_terms.definition: With flexible designs, customers choose coverage most relevant to them, skewing the risk pool.
Policyholder cancels after a brief period, before the insurer recovers acquisition costs.

subsections.title: Meeting Customer Needs
bullets: Attractive in markets with high medical costs and limited/no State cover or employer plans.
Allows small regular premiums to protect against large unexpected costs.
Especially valuable for families (single family-level deductible).
Even comprehensive MME has maximum limits → policyholder remains exposed to very large costs above the cap.

3.summary:
Private medical insurance

Benefits and features
• Cover dependent on State benefits, may be indemnity or supplement to State cover.
• Exclusions may apply to cover.
• Benefits may be paid to care provider or patient to reimburse for costs incurred.
• Premiums annually renewable, rated by age, gender and medical history. Rating may be restricted where PMI is compulsory.

Insurer’s perspective
• Claims – If indemnity, risk of very large claim amounts, can be managed via health maintenance organisation (HMOs) or preferred provider organisations (PPOs), these restrict choice over care provision. Claim rates may be higher than expected, pre-existing condition clauses and underwriting can help manage if permitted. Managed via using GPs as gatekeepers for claims, excesses / coinsurance / NCDs to reduce claim frequency and amounts, incentives for ‘healthy’ activities.
• Underwriting – key area for managing PMI risks where permitted by legislation.

Meeting customer needs
• Where State provide healthcare, PMI can provide faster treatment, choice over the hospital or doctor or perceived higher quality care.
• Where no State healthcare, insurance may be compulsory. Shortfalls in State care can drive the evolution of PMI.

Health cash plans

Benefits and features
• Annually renewable, defined cash benefit products, primarily available in the UK.
• Healthcare events covered may include dental, optical, physiotherapy, maternity, hospitalisation, recuperation, hearing aids and consultations.
• Cover purchased in ‘units’. A waiting period may apply, particularly for maternity benefits.

Insurer’s perspective
• Claim size – Fixed benefits so reduced risk of very large claims vs indemnity cover. Risk of more claims than expected managed by NCDs and limits on claims. Risk of claims being paid for more than cost to the individual managed by limiting claim to max proportion of the medical bill.
• Premiums – Low cost cover requires renewals to cover expenses and profit.

Meeting customer needs
• Helps to offer certain private treatments where PMI is too expensive.
• Can work alongside PMI to offer additional cover eg optical or dental cover.
• Can provide cash for overnight stays in hospital.

Major medical expense insurance

Benefits and features
• Developed to cover high medical expenses.
• May have to cover specified services to be defined as ‘major medical insurance’.
• Deductibles and coinsurance used to deter smaller claims.

Insurer’s perspective
• Claims – not ‘true’ indemnity as use deductibles / coinsurance but risk of very large claims. Managed by policy limits or reinsurance.
• External factors – product design affected by legislation, regulation and political changes. Can be hard to quantify issues given lag on experience analysis.
• Flexibility – more choice demanded by consumers (eg choice over deductibles, coinsurance, benefits covered) but increases complexity for insurers (eg increased anti-selection if benefit choice offered, harder to understand product).
• Lapses – some issues with short-term lapses.

Meeting customer needs
• Particularly attractive where very high medical costs, lack of State healthcare and those with no access to employer-sponsored health plans.
• Maximum limits may mean policyholders are still exposed to very large claim costs.
• Cover can be confusing, legislation around minimum level of cover can help ensure cover meets basic needs.

4.title: Product Analysis

subsections.title: Overview of Chapter 4
bullets: Chapter 4 extends product analysis from Chapters 2 and 3 by examining: (1) needs of other customers — intermediaries, employers, and low-income individuals; (2) interaction between health insurance and State provision; (3) higher-order insurer risk considerations; (4) bundling vs unbundling; (5) impact of unit-linked wrappers.
The first three topics were introduced in SP1 and are developed further here.

The central thread is that product features must be traceable to specific needs — of customers, intermediaries, employers, or the insurer — and that the risk and regulatory environment fundamentally shapes what products are viable and how they are designed.

subsections.title: Intermediaries Needs
bullets: The salesperson/broker primarily aims to maximise income by maximising successful sales. Their key needs are: clear contract wording (helps customers understand and reduces lapses); comprehensive and attractive benefits; value for money; competitive pricing; ability to bundle/unbundle products; remuneration (commission/bonus); hassle-free underwriting; control over the customer relationship; compliance with sales regulation requirements.
Clear policy wording enables customers to understand and appreciate the product, easing the sales process and reducing subsequent lapsing.

Hassle-free underwriting (especially in group business): high underwriting requirements lengthen the sales process and increase broker workload, deterring sales. Minimising administration around a sale is important to the employer/broker.

Control over the customer relationship: brokers want to maintain close post-sale relationships to capitalise on future opportunities — upgrades, cover changes according to varying needs, provider switches (broker sales), and new products.
Sales regulation requirements are set by local regulators; details are covered in Chapter 25.

key_terms.definition: Grouping together various benefits into one packaged product sold as a single unit.
Splitting benefits under a product into separate products that can be sold individually.

exam_qa.q: Why does a broker want hassle-free underwriting on group business?
exam_qa.a: The employer/broker wishes to minimise the administration required around a sale. High underwriting requirements (or low free cover limits) mean many questions must be answered, which lengthens the sales process, may deter sales, and increases the work the broker must carry out.

exam_qa.q: Why does a broker want control over the customer relationship after a group sale?
exam_qa.a: The broker wants to maintain a close relationship to capitalise on future selling opportunities — upgrading cover, providing other benefits, changing cover to meet varying needs, or switching provider. This maximises the chances of the customer returning to them for new products.

subsections.title: Employers Needs
bullets: An employer (or other group) will often have needs in the provision of health and care insurance products, or the insurance of contractual obligations, to employees (or associated individuals).

Employer needs include: fulfilling statutory responsibilities; minimising sickness absence; attracting and retaining staff; providing benefits to employees; protecting the business against the sickness absence of key staff; tax efficiency in the provision of employee benefits; maximising value (group cover is typically cheaper than individual cover); ease of assessment and payment; avoidance of discrimination.
Employer staff see these benefits as particularly attractive in jurisdictions where there is limited or no corresponding State provision.

In Switzerland, it is compulsory for employers to offer PMI as there is no State healthcare provision. In the USA, employers often offer discounted PMI or major medical insurance given the high cost of individual healthcare.

key_terms.definition: Insurance arranged by an employer (or other group) for a group of employees or members, typically cheaper per head than equivalent individual cover.
Maximum benefit amount in a group scheme up to which individual underwriting is not required.

exam_qa.q: List the key needs of an employer when purchasing group health insurance.
exam_qa.a: Fulfilling statutory responsibilities; minimising sickness absence; attracting and retaining staff; providing employee benefits; protecting against key staff absence; tax efficiency; maximising value (group cover cheaper than individual); ease of assessment and payment; avoidance of discrimination.

subsections.title: Microinsurance: Meeting the Needs of Low-Income Customers
bullets: In many developing countries, health and care insurance products may be available but the cost of coverage is prohibitive for low-income individuals, who may form a significant proportion of the population.

Microinsurance is insurance targeted at those who are working but with low incomes. The ILO defines it as a mechanism to protect poor people against risk (accident, illness, death in family, natural disasters etc) in exchange for insurance premium payments tailored to their needs, income, and level of risk.
Characterised by limited benefits and very low premiums (as little as $1/month) for affordability. It is not a distinct product type — it is the target market (low income) that defines it.
Most relevant SA1 variants: health microinsurance (hospitalisation, primary care, maternity) and disability microinsurance (inability to work due to illness or injury).

Microinsurance enables low-income individuals — more vulnerable to adverse events with fewer savings — to purchase some financial protection, avoiding the need to sell assets/livestock or rely on expensive money-lenders.

It is often sold alongside microfinance loans; banks may require insurance before extending credit. Some provision of health cover is especially reassuring to families in countries with limited State welfare.

Distribution: the partner-agent model (insurer uses local agents such as microfinance institutions to distribute; agents earn commission) is most common and generates benefits for all parties. An alternative is the full-service model, where local agents run the scheme through health centres or hospitals they own and run.
Rating is generally based on pooling or community rating; in some countries it can be compulsory.

Microinsurance is particularly important for the developing world and is well developed in India and parts of Africa (e.g., Senegal, Bangladesh, Vietnam). Some jurisdictions (e.g., South Africa) have reduced regulatory barriers; combined with technology, this makes microinsurance more viable for some insurers.
Challenges for developed-world insurers operating in this space are discussed in Chapter 21.

key_terms.definition: Insurance targeted at low-income working individuals; characterised by very low premiums, limited benefits, and community or pooled rating.
Microinsurance distribution model where an insurer (partner) distributes via local agents (e.g., microfinance institutions) who earn commission on sales.
Microinsurance model where local agents run the scheme directly through health centres or hospitals they own and operate.
Same premium charged to all policyholders in a group regardless of individual risk factors.

exam_qa.q: Why is microinsurance particularly important for low-income individuals in developing countries?
exam_qa.a: Low-income individuals are more vulnerable to adverse events and have fewer savings to support themselves in times of need. Without health insurance, they may have to use savings, sell assets or livestock, or rely on expensive money-lenders. Microinsurance provides affordable financial protection and is especially reassuring where State welfare support is limited.

exam_qa.q: Describe the partner-agent model for distributing microinsurance.
exam_qa.a: The insurer (partner) increases its new business volumes via local agents (such as microfinance institutions). The local agents earn commission on sales, driving economic growth. The local population benefits from having health insurance at a lower cost than a traditional product. This model is generally considered to generate the most benefits for all parties.

subsections.title: Introduction
bullets: The role of the State in insuring the population against the financial burden of being unable to work due to poor health varies greatly across markets. As a consequence, the role of health and care insurance also varies greatly between countries.

State benefits affect both consumer demand for insurance products and the design of products within a market. The level and extent of State healthcare and benefits are therefore important considerations when designing health insurance products.

subsections.title: What Could the State Provide?
bullets: The State may provide: sick pay for a limited period; maternity/paternity pay; longer-term income for those unable to work due to sickness or disability; financial support for carers.

UK State benefits currently include: Attendance Allowance (elderly requiring care at home); Carer's Allowance (carers spending at least 35 hours/week caring); Personal Independence Payment (PIP, for people with a disability); Statutory Sick Pay (SSP, employer-paid to employees unable to work due to sickness, up to 28 weeks); Employment and Support Allowance (ESA, paid while unable to work due to sickness/disability and not entitled to SSP, varying by duration of illness, impact on ability to work, and readiness to return to work).

key_terms.definition: UK State benefit paid by employers to employees unable to work due to sickness, for up to a maximum of 28 weeks.
UK State benefit paid to those unable to work due to sickness or disability who are not entitled to SSP; amount varies by duration, work impact, and return-to-work readiness.
UK State benefit paid to people with a disability.

subsections.title: Government Agenda
bullets: Government healthcare policy can have a huge impact on insurer success. The level of State benefits affects the perceived needs of the potential health insurance purchaser.
Government actions may directly affect health insurance providers through: regulations; taxation; communication (e.g., public statements criticising the actions of insurers).

Political philosophies range from requiring all benefits to be earned (no free entitlement to healthcare or income support during incapacity) to providing cover for an individual's whole life (cradle to grave). The UK generally offers cradle-to-grave healthcare, though some elements such as prescription charges are means-tested. Switzerland has no free State health services but compulsory private medical insurance for all residents.

Political uncertainty is a significant source of risk for health insurers. Even where both major parties support the current system, a future government change could fundamentally alter demand. Political uncertainty has the greatest impact on PMI (sensitive to changes in State care availability) and LTCI (uncertain future State funding for long-term care).
Insurers must take measures to mitigate the effects of political uncertainty.

key_terms.definition: Political philosophy of providing comprehensive healthcare and income support throughout an individual's entire life, funded through general taxation (e.g., UK NHS model).
Risk that a future change in government policy fundamentally alters the level of State benefits and therefore demand for private health insurance products.

exam_qa.q: Which health insurance products are most exposed to political uncertainty, and why?
exam_qa.a: PMI, because changes to State care availability directly affect demand for private medical cover; and LTCI, because the extent of future State funding for long-term care is highly uncertain. Both products are highly sensitive to the level of State provision in their respective areas.

subsections.title: Are Insurers Competing or Complementing?
bullets: Where State provision is limited or absent, insurers are generally complementing (or substituting for) the State. Comprehensive private provision may in turn affect the State's decision about expanding its role.
Where State provision is comprehensive, insurers are ostensibly competing with the State — asking the public to pay for healthcare provision after they have already paid through the taxation system.
Private hospitals also compete with the State for resources: consultants and nurses may leave the State system to work privately. This could weaken the State healthcare system.

However, individuals choosing to pay privately relieve the State of some responsibility and shorten queues. Governments may recognise this by offering tax relief or other incentives (e.g., Ireland offers tax relief on PMI premiums; the UK does not, having a comprehensive free State system).
Political issues surrounding health insurance are examined further later in the course; exam marks are best gained by presenting both sides of the argument.

key_terms.definition: Where private insurers fill gaps left by limited State provision, acting as a substitute or supplement rather than a competitor.
Where comprehensive State provision exists and private insurers ask policyholders to pay twice — once via taxation and once via premiums — for overlapping coverage.

exam_qa.q: In what sense are private medical insurers both competing with and complementing the State?
exam_qa.a: Competing: private hospitals compete with the State for staff and resources; insurers ask people to pay again for coverage already funded through tax. Complementing: those who use private care relieve the State of some burden, shorten queues, and reduce pressure on State resources. Governments may recognise this by offering tax relief on PMI premiums.

subsections.title: Future Demographics — The Demographic Time Bomb
bullets: State systems face mounting structural pressure where: there are constraints on the amount of taxation that can be levied; the country's population is ageing; persons are surviving longer in ill-health; there is a proportionally declining workforce (taxpayers). This situation is not uncommon in many developed countries.

This demographic time-bomb means expenditure rises while revenue is constrained. Governments must either raise taxes (politically unpopular) or reduce State benefits. Any reduction in State benefits creates opportunities for health insurers.

In the UK, demographic pressure combined with rising medical costs has placed severe financial and organisational pressure on the NHS — large provider deficits, missed waiting-time targets, and staff pressures from rising demand and constrained resources.
The impact of demographic issues on insurers is examined further in Chapter 6.

key_terms.definition: The structural fiscal problem arising from an ageing population, rising healthcare costs, and a declining working-age tax base, placing State healthcare systems under mounting financial pressure.

exam_qa.q: How does the demographic time-bomb create opportunities for health insurers?
exam_qa.a: As the population ages and healthcare costs rise, governments face pressure to reduce State benefits (since raising taxes is unpopular and constrained). Any reduction in State provision increases the gap that private health insurance can fill, driving demand for products such as PMI, IP, and LTCI.

subsections.title: Taxation of State Benefits
bullets: The taxation of State benefits may vary by country. In some countries, income-type benefits paid by the State may be taxed in the same way as earned income. Alternatively, State benefits may be exempt from any income tax.
The tax treatment of State benefits affects their net value to the recipient and therefore the relative attractiveness of private insurance cover.

subsections.title: Introduction
bullets: This section gives an overview of risk considerations for insurers relating to product design and pricing. The management of risk in this context is developed further in Chapter 8.

Subject SP1 described risks faced by health insurers and specific risks for the four main health products. This chapter examines in more detail some of the most important risks, many of which apply regardless of the particular health product being written.

The insurer needs to model the risk when setting premium rates and when establishing suitable reserves and capital requirements. The most relevant data is the insurer's own experience, subject to credibility and level of detail captured.

subsections.title: Knowledge of Risk and Availability of Statistics
bullets: The insurer must model risk accurately for premium rating and for reserving and capital requirements. The most relevant data source is the insurer's own experience; applicability depends on credibility and detail of data captured.
An understanding of risks is fundamental to all actuarial investigations: pricing, reserving, business planning, launching a new product, setting investment strategy.

The insurer's own data may not be used if: there is insufficient volume for credibility (especially for new products); the data is no longer relevant due to changes in product design, underwriting standards, or underlying population.
For premium rating, the insurer must also predict the likely volume and mix of new and renewing business.

Data for a LTCI product would typically be split by: age; gender; smoker status; type of product (pre-funded or immediate needs); size of benefit; distribution channel; location; underwriting status (standard rates or special terms).

Reasons why risk cells may not be priced separately include: insufficient data per cell; inadequate systems; seller opposition to data collection; market acceptability (consumers unwilling to accept certain rating factors); legal/social constraints (e.g., use of gender or age may be discriminatory); lack of competitive pressure; difficulty allocating fixed expenses and commission; need for consistency across cells to avoid anomalous rates.

As a result, cross-subsidies between risk cells are common. UK PMI example: before 2012 it was unusual for PMI insurers to rate by gender despite different male/female claim experience, as the whole market used unisex rates and there was no anti-selection risk. The European Court of Justice mandated unisex rates from 21 December 2012.

Also in UK PMI: younger policyholders have long subsidised older ones. Insurers accepted this because PMI persistency rates are high, so charging younger people slightly more (with the surplus reducing premiums for older lives) was commercially viable.

The estimation of future healthcare experience is complex, depending on external influences such as claims consciousness and economic welfare. Where the actuary sees potential for wide variation, appropriate margins must be incorporated; stochastic modelling may assist.
Claim consciousness — the general awareness customers have of their ability and willingness to claim — is generally increasing. The factors contributing to this are covered in Chapter 6.

key_terms.definition: A homogeneous sub-group of the portfolio defined by combinations of rating factors (e.g., age band, gender, occupation) used in claims experience analysis and premium rating.
Where one group of policyholders effectively subsidises another because premiums do not precisely reflect the risk of each individual risk cell.
The general awareness customers have of their ability to make claims and their willingness to do so; generally increasing over time.
The risk that the model used to price or reserve is incorrect in structure or assumptions, leading to inadequate premiums or reserves.
The risk that the assumptions used in an otherwise correct model are wrong.

exam_qa.q: Describe the circumstances in which an insurer's own data might not be used to price a healthcare product.
exam_qa.a: The insurer's own data may not be used if there is insufficient volume for credibility — given the complexity and uncertainty of health claims, a very large volume may be required before data is sufficiently credible. For a new product there may be no data at all. Data may also be unusable if it is no longer relevant due to changes in product design, underwriting standards, or the underlying population.

exam_qa.q: Why may premiums not be differentiated between the risk cells identified in a claims experience analysis?
exam_qa.a: Possible reasons include: insufficient data within each cell; inadequate systems to capture relevant factors; opposition from sellers to the data collection required; consumer unwillingness to accept certain rating factors; legal or social constraints (e.g., gender or age may be deemed discriminatory); lack of competitive pressure to price precisely; difficulty allocating fixed expenses and commission to each cell; and the need for consistency so that rates do not appear anomalous.

subsections.title: Avoidance of Anti-Selection
bullets: Insurance is a promise to pay a benefit on occurrence of an event during the policy term. The insurer bases the premium on the estimated probability of the insured event. However, the insured may know more about their own morbidity risk than the insurer, and in many types of healthcare insurance the applicant also has choice over the level of cover.

Anti-selection arises because policyholders have choice over: (a) whether to take out cover, and (b) in many cases, the level of cover taken out. Those who believe they have a greater risk of claiming — where that risk is not reflected in the premium or underwriting conditions — will be more disposed to take out a product and to take out a high level of cover.

Mitigation: the insurer must ask all appropriate questions explicitly at the application stage and require medical information before accepting the risk. A vague catch-all question is rarely sufficient because: policyholders may not know what factors affect their risk of claiming; they may not remember all relevant medical conditions; and they may not be willing to spend the time required. These failures may not constitute deliberate misrepresentation.
Anti-selection may also be present during the policy term if options are available to extend or increase cover.

Costing anti-selection: if using own data, the effect of anti-selection is already embedded in the claims data — only potential trends in the degree of anti-selection need to be incorporated in pricing. If using external data, the extent of anti-selection is unknown and an explicit allowance must be built in.

Regardless of how it is costed, anti-selection must also be controlled — otherwise its impact may escalate over time. Example: a small IP insurer asking few medical questions will attract higher-risk lives; if the rest of the market tightens underwriting, the insurer's experience will worsen progressively unless it strengthens its own underwriting.

key_terms.definition: The tendency for higher-risk individuals to be more likely to take out insurance (or take out higher levels of cover), because they know more about their own risk than the insurer does.

A vague question (e.g., are there any other circumstances that may affect the risk?) intended to capture undisclosed information; rarely sufficient on its own to protect the insurer from non-disclosure.
Option allowing the insured to increase cover at specified times or life events without further underwriting; a potential source of anti-selection during the policy term.

exam_qa.q: Why is the catch-all question on a health insurance proposal form rarely sufficient to protect an insurer from non-disclosure?
exam_qa.a: The average policyholder will not know what factors affect their risk of claiming. They may not remember all medical conditions they have experienced, and this vague question is unlikely to jog their memory. They may also not be willing to spend the time to consider and answer fully. These are valid reasons and do not constitute deliberate misrepresentation — so the insurer must ask specific questions instead.

exam_qa.q: How should anti-selection be treated in pricing when using the insurer's own data vs external data?
exam_qa.a: If using own data: the effect of anti-selection is already embedded in the claims experience, so only potential trends in the degree of anti-selection (not the current level) need to be allowed for. If using external data: the extent of anti-selection is unknown and an explicit, prudent allowance must be built into the pricing basis. In both cases, anti-selection must also be controlled, not just costed.

subsections.title: Costing of Moral Hazard
bullets: Moral hazard relates to claims behaviour where individuals undertake more potentially claim-inducing activities because they are insured. Examples: using private rather than State healthcare (PMI); seeking non-domiciliary care because LTCI is held; remaining off work longer with IP cover; getting earlier scans with CI cover.
This behaviour is not necessarily immoral — it is a natural consequence of having insurance. However, the insurer must be aware of it when pricing using population statistics.

To price for moral hazard, the insurer must assess its prevalence within the insured population relative to the general population experience. If using own data, the effect is already embedded — only trends in moral hazard need to be allowed for.

Fraud (deliberate non-disclosure, falsifying the nature or extent of a claim) is sometimes attributed to moral hazard but should be treated as a separate aspect. Where fraud is present, the insurer should investigate and can legally reject the claim. Industry bodies (e.g., Health Insurance Counter Fraud Group in the UK) facilitate sharing of anti-fraud approaches.
Anti-fraud efforts must be balanced against the distress caused by questioning valid claims, particularly for seriously ill policyholders.
The pricing actuary or underwriter must identify and quantify (or mitigate) all sources of moral hazard behaviour when designing new products or undertaking pricing reviews.

key_terms.definition: The tendency for insured individuals to behave in ways that increase the likelihood or cost of a claim because the financial consequences are borne by the insurer.
Deliberate non-disclosure or falsification of a claim; treated separately from moral hazard and legally justifies claim rejection.

exam_qa.q: How does moral hazard differ from fraud in a health insurance context?
exam_qa.a: Moral hazard is the natural tendency for insured individuals to behave differently (in ways that increase claims) because they are covered — this is not necessarily immoral and is a foreseeable consequence of insurance that must be priced. Fraud involves deliberate non-disclosure or falsification of a claim and is a separate issue; it can be legally investigated and, where confirmed, the claim can be rejected.

subsections.title: Retrospective Regulatory / Legislative Changes
bullets: Legislation or regulation may occasionally be imposed on a portfolio already in force, affecting the nature and/or future cost of the insurer-policyholder relationship. This may impact the risk itself (retrospectively) or the way the policy is serviced (adding to expense).

Where insurance has been purchased on guaranteed terms, the insurer can only meet additional costs from surplus capital. Where the insurance is reviewable, the premium/benefit relationship can be changed at the next periodic review (or next renewal for one-year policies).

Even if legislation does not change, if the interpretation of legislation and policy wording changes over time (e.g., through court decisions on disputed claims setting a precedent), this will have a similar effect.

key_terms.definition: Regulation or legislation imposed on in-force policies that alters the cost or nature of the insurer-policyholder relationship without the insurer having been able to price for it.

subsections.title: Guarantees and Options — Adequate Costing
bullets: Seven key principles when providing business with guarantees: (1) ensure there is a customer need; (2) price as accurately as possible, projecting a range of potential outcomes; (3) charge cost of capital to the product if possible; (4) obtain sound reinsurance, building cost into the product if necessary; (5) ensure marketing and policy literature clearly describes the guarantees; (6) ensure the sales process explains any guarantees and their implications for premiums and benefits; (7) ensure adequate reserves are in place when business is written.

Where a contract contains any element of guarantee, the insurer must establish the cost using stochastic modelling to estimate the probability of the guarantee coming into the money. The cost of necessary reserves (which may earn a lower rate of return than the overall product yield) should be communicated to buyers of guaranteed products.

Options on health and care products include: renewal options on short-term contracts (right to renew; insurer cannot cancel, but can change premiums); Guaranteed Insurability Options (GIOs, increase cover at regular intervals or life events without further underwriting); CI buy-back (after a CI claim, reinstates life cover, usually after at least 12 months; subsequent CI claims excluded); spouse insurability options (include spouse without medical evidence at specified intervals/life events).

The 12-month waiting period on CI buy-back life cover is justified because mortality in the months following a critical illness is more uncertain and heavier than usual; the waiting period protects the insurer from this uncertainty and keeps the price manageable.

Options must also be modelled to estimate the frequency of exercise and the advantage to the policyholder; some offset is available for those who will not exercise the option (due to apathy or forgetfulness) even when it is to their advantage.
Options that may be taken up at the policyholder's choice operate in a similar way to guarantees and must be modelled using stochastic methods where possible to ascertain their true cost.

key_terms.definition: Option allowing the insured to increase cover at certain specified times or life events without further underwriting; a form of guarantee that must be costed.
Option that reinstates life cover following a CI claim (typically after a waiting period of at least 12 months); subsequent CI claims are excluded.
Modelling approach that projects a range of possible outcomes and their probabilities, particularly useful for costing guarantees and options.

exam_qa.q: List the seven key principles an insurer should follow when providing business with guarantees.
exam_qa.a: (1) Ensure there is a customer need; (2) price as accurately as possible, projecting a range of outcomes; (3) charge cost of capital to the product if possible; (4) obtain sound reinsurance, building cost in if necessary; (5) ensure marketing/policy literature clearly describes the guarantees; (6) ensure the sales process explains the guarantee and its premium/benefit implications; (7) ensure adequate reserves are in place when business is written.

exam_qa.q: Why is there a waiting period on the reinstatement of life cover following a CI buy-back?
exam_qa.a: Mortality in the months immediately following a critical illness is more uncertain and heavier than prior to the event. By excluding death claims during this period, the insurer is protected from this heightened uncertainty, which also keeps the price of the buy-back option at a manageable level.

subsections.title: Competitors Actions
bullets: Competition is a reasonable guide to collective wisdom, but an individual insurer must assess risk fully against its own circumstances — available capital, target market, method of distribution — before accepting any major changes in product design or pricing that competitors are making.
The financial effect of standing alone (following a strategy not in line with the majority of competitors) must be carefully modelled, including any negative aspects.

subsections.title: External Advice
bullets: Changes proposed by reinsurers or consultants — new products, new methods of servicing, outsourcing, new forms of distribution, or new territories — must undergo a rigorous cost/benefit/risk analysis before adoption.

subsections.title: Customer Preferences
bullets: The customer must weigh the simplicity of an all-in-one bundled product (single proposal form, possible expense savings) against the ability to shop around for the cheapest provider of each individual cover.

A bundled package may not exactly match the customer's requirements in terms of benefit structure, but it may be priced more cheaply by reducing overlaps. A broker will generally assist by acquiring the best of breed — the most appropriate product weighing cost against benefits and conditions for each component.

Bundled products should in general be cheaper than buying each benefit separately, because: expenses are lower (economies of scale, reduced administration); underwriting is simpler and cheaper (single proposal form, only one GP report); benefit overlaps are removed; bundled products may be less open to anti-selection; pricing margins may be reduced as there is less overall uncertainty about future experience.
However, in a competitive market for separate products, the bundled package may not provide the best value for money.

Benefit overlaps in combined products: CI and IP — conditions triggering both would receive only the CI lump sum and IP cover ceases; the premium saving versus holding both separately can be significant. PMI and health cash plan — overlaps include daily cash while in State hospital, outpatient consultations/diagnostics, physiotherapy/osteopathy, and nursing care while convalescing; any overlap not required by the customer can be removed.

Menu-based/modular products offer a middle ground: customer chooses from a range of benefits (often a core benefit plus optional add-ons), getting cost advantages of bundling with more precise needs-matching. Risks include: increased anti-selection; higher system/admin costs; higher advice/acquisition costs; customer confusion leading to complaints or lapses; risk of customer making a wrong decision and blaming the insurer.

key_terms.definition: A product where the customer selects from a range of benefit options (often with a compulsory core benefit), combining the cost advantages of bundling with some individual tailoring.
Broker approach of selecting the most appropriate product for each individual benefit from across the market, rather than purchasing a combined package from one insurer.

exam_qa.q: Why should bundled products generally be cheaper than buying each benefit separately?
exam_qa.a: Expenses are lower due to economies of scale and reduced administration; underwriting is simpler and cheaper (single proposal form, one GP report); any benefit overlaps are removed and reflected in the price; bundled products may be less open to anti-selection; and pricing margins can be reduced as there is less overall uncertainty about future experience.

exam_qa.q: What are the main problems for an insurer in offering a menu-based product to individual customers?
exam_qa.a: Offering choice increases anti-selection risk (customers select benefits they expect to use most). New systems may be needed, increasing costs. More advice is required, increasing acquisition costs. Product complexity increases the risk of customers not understanding their purchase, leading to complaints and lapses. There is also a danger of customers making wrong decisions and holding the insurer responsible.

subsections.title: Broker Preferences
bullets: The broker, acting on behalf of a customer, wants to demonstrate value by identifying client needs and securing the best deal (justifying commission). Brokers therefore generally prefer the unbundled approach, keeping in touch with several insurers.
Brokers will be mindful of the cost savings of the bundled approach and will recommend bundling where the benefit structure suits the customer.
General rule: bundling works best for direct sales; unbundling is usually preferred when a broker is involved.

subsections.title: Pricing Actuary Preferences
bullets: Bundling allows the actuary to offset risks and margins in pricing. Well-understood benefits can be priced confidently standalone; new or uncertain benefits (e.g., dental, optical when first introduced) are less dangerous within a bundle because margins from established benefits provide a buffer if pricing proves inadequate.

Two ways to combine risks under a single product wrapper: (1) Either/or basis — benefit paid on the first claim event, cover ceases thereafter (e.g., accelerated CI — death or CI, whichever occurs first). Drawback: no death benefit if CI event has already been paid. (2) Both risks basis — each benefit paid independently, contract continues until the last benefit is paid (e.g., joint life CI with both lives covered; waiver of premium on LTCI). Higher aggregate cost but no cover ceases on first event.

CI buy-back was developed to address the drawback of the either/or approach — it reinstates life cover following a CI claim, usually after a waiting period of at least 12 months, with subsequent CI claims excluded.

The actuary must also consider how claims behaviour may differ in a bundled vs standalone product. Example: if both IP and CI policies are held, payment of a CI lump sum may reduce the claimant's financial incentive to return to work, potentially prolonging the IP claim.

Data used in pricing separate products may need to be adjusted when pricing a bundled product, and vice versa, as the bundled product may appeal to a different market and be sold via different channels.

key_terms.definition: Bundling arrangement where the benefit is paid on the first claim event and cover ceases thereafter (e.g., accelerated CI paying on death or CI, whichever is first).
Bundling arrangement where each benefit is paid independently and the contract continues until the last benefit has been paid.

exam_qa.q: Describe the two ways in which risks can be combined under a single product wrapper, giving an example of each.
exam_qa.a: Either/or basis: benefit paid on first claim event, cover ceases (example: accelerated CI — pays on death or CI, whichever is first; no death benefit if CI claim already paid). Both risks basis: each benefit paid independently, contract continues until the last benefit is paid (example: joint life CI where both lives are covered, or waiver of premium on a LTCI policy).

subsections.title: Administrator Preferences
bullets: Administrators include: insurance company staff handling new business, in-force administration, and claims; brokers acting as go-between; employing groups doing some record-keeping; third-party administrators to whom the insurer or employer outsources work.

Bundled products can create systems problems if systems were not designed with these structures in mind — e.g., the possibility of a sum insured being paid while the contract remains in force, and different benefit cells/premiums on a single policy record.

Features of a system designed for individual products may not handle bundled structures correctly. For example, a system designed for CI benefits will terminate cover and stop collecting premiums on CI payment; under a bundled CI and IP product, the CI benefit ceases but the IP cover continues (possibly at a lower premium).
Removal of benefit overlaps adds further complexity to system requirements.
Advantage: capturing policyholder details and underwriting only once for a bundled product is administratively advantageous, assuming the system links all benefits to one customer record.

subsections.title: Insurer Preferences
bullets: Bundled products provide the insurer with the opportunity for increased income — the customer buys several benefits from one insurer rather than just one. Administrative costs should also be lower.
The biggest problem with bundled products is the risk that the package does not meet customer needs precisely — customers have different needs, so one package will not suit all.

The relative merits of bundling/unbundling depend on distribution channel and market preferences. These will change over time — insurers will sometimes bundle products previously sold separately, or unbundle benefits from a product and start selling them separately.

subsections.title: Introduction
bullets: Unit-linked health products were introduced in Subject SP1. The main advantages of designing health products in a unit-linked rather than conventional form are: (1) the risk is shared with the customer rather than being entirely borne by the insurer, making it easier and more acceptable to customers to review premiums and benefits; (2) it allows for more complex benefit design; (3) it permits more accurate pricing.
The main disadvantage is that unit-linked products are difficult for the average customer to understand.

key_terms.definition: A product structure where premiums are invested in a notional unit fund, and charges for insurance cover are deducted from units; makes risk-sharing and premium review more transparent and flexible.

subsections.title: Reviewability
bullets: Long-term health insurance products carry significant uncertainty about future experience. A common protection for the insurer is reviewable premiums — the right to change premiums if experience (claims or expenses) differs from that assumed in pricing. Premiums tend to be reviewed every five years or so.

Practical difficulties with traditional reviewable premiums: premium increases may be unacceptable or confusing to policyholders who do not understand the calculation; policyholder reasonable expectation may develop if premiums are not reviewed for many years; selective lapsing — healthy policyholders leave after an increase, worsening the remaining risk pool; the revised premium can be difficult to calculate correctly in practice.

A unit-linked format makes the review process easier to calculate, communicate, and record. The various risk elements (claims experience, expenses, investment return, persistency) can be assessed regularly to decide on the premium/benefit relationship for the forthcoming period.

Calculation: under unit-linked, the actuary simply adjusts the relevant charges (e.g., increases the morbidity charge by 10%). Under traditional policies, premiums must be recalculated using cashflow/profit-testing techniques with revised assumptions.

Communication: customers expect regular reviews as part of the fund-sufficiency check. They are not forced to increase premiums but understand that failure to do so may result in cover running out sooner or a reduced fund value.

Records: unit-linked system changes are simple parameter adjustments; traditional systems may need to be altered to handle premium changes, and the original pricing basis must be maintained and updated at each review.

Risk-sharing: under traditional policies, even where premiums are reviewable, all risks other than those subject to review (e.g., investment risk) remain with the insurer. Under most unit-linked policies, the customer bears the investment risk (the fund falls if returns are poor) and a degree of expense risk.
