Renewable/short-term business: abandonment means not renewing at the end of the term.

subsections.title: Market Perception
bullets: Poorly handled withdrawal causes brand damage, media attention, and increased lapse rates. Existing customers may fear financial instability, question value for money, or feel trapped (especially on policies with small/no surrender values).
For long-term products (CI, IP, LTCI), fallout is relatively limited since the insurer must honour existing contracts; distributor concern is more likely than policyholder concern.

For short-term renewable products, closure is more disruptive. If withdrawal is due to losses, the replacement product will likely offer poorer value and the transition must be managed carefully. PMI insurers often use incentives to encourage transfer rather than forced switches.

subsections.title: Implications of a Declining Portfolio
bullets: Critical mass for premium review: a shrinking portfolio may become statistically unsustainable for experience analysis; the new line's experience may not be directly applicable.

Selective deterioration: healthier lives who can obtain cover elsewhere will leave; those unable to switch due to poor health remain, causing a spiral of higher claims → premium increases → more lapses → worse experience.

subsections.title: Reinsurer Reaction
bullets: If a product is loss-making, the reinsurer will generally support profitability improvement strategies or cancel the treaty for new business.
The insurer may need to provide the reinsurer an opportunity to recover losses via cession of new/similar lines; reinsurance treaty terms must be considered when deciding whether to abandon.
Lessons from the failed product must be applied to any successor.

subsections.title: Reasons for Changing the Contract
bullets: Competitive pressure: other insurers offer better benefit packages, prompting upgrades to maintain attractiveness and avoid lapse-and-re-entry. Lapse-and-re-entry is most damaging early in the policy term when the earned asset share is negative.
Financial performance: the product is not behaving as expected; reviewability clauses are used to adjust premiums up or down.
Correcting errors: errors in policy wording need to be rectified.

subsections.title: Legality
bullets: The insurer may improve (but not worsen) a guaranteed contract without invalidating it; increasing premiums on guaranteed contracts is generally not permissible.
For reviewable contracts, the reviewability clause must be carefully worded to preserve legality post-amendment.
For annually renewable policies, the contract technically renews each year so changes are legally possible — but must be marketed and worded clearly to avoid regulatory or court challenge.

subsections.title: Market Perception
bullets: Market view of contract changes is influenced by what other insurers are doing — acting out of step invites suspicion or disapproval.
Radical changes can alternatively be seen as innovation and generate market share gains before competitors catch up.

UK examples: BUPA's individually priced PMI (lifestyle factors) was initially sceptical but has since become standard; Norwich Union Healthcare's NHS cash benefit product attracted political criticism but remains in operation.

subsections.title: Treating Customers Fairly (TCF)
bullets: Policyholders' reasonable expectations under reviewable contracts are difficult to gauge — a key TCF consideration.
Customers must understand from the outset the distinction between guaranteed and reviewable contracts; some companies annually remind customers of the reviewability.
TCF requirements and their interpretation vary significantly by jurisdiction.

subsections.title: Administration
bullets: Changed policies require systems to retain a historical record of cover applicable at each date — needed for future claims validation.

Administrative burden is reduced if existing and new business conditions are changed simultaneously, so all in-force policies share the same benefit structure at any given time, simplifying claims assessment.
Precise historical records are also important as evidence in disputed claims.

subsections.title: Choice or Compulsion
bullets: Under reviewable contracts, if the insurer exercises the right to increase premiums, the policyholder faces three options: accept, reduce benefits, or lapse.
Those more likely to accept increases tend to be higher-risk individuals (e.g. with a new medical condition, or currently claiming) — the insurer must account for selective lapsing.
Contract alterations are harder to impose where health cover is a rider to a larger product (e.g. whole of life or endowment policy).

subsections.title: Projected Volumes and Profitability
bullets: Any changes require the actuary to update the model office with new assumptions, including volume forecasts for new business and business retention rates.
In healthcare, given potential for outcome variability, the actuary should revise stochastic assessments of likely outcomes.
Even seemingly cost-neutral benefit additions should be statistically evaluated for potential adverse impact on profitability and solvency.

subsections.title: Reinsurance
bullets: Any premium/benefit change affects reinsurance arrangements and must be discussed with the reinsurer in advance.
Most reinsurance treaties permit premium variation within certain limits without requiring full renegotiation.

subsections.title: Overview
bullets: The goal is to make claims less likely or less severe.
Each initiative should be subject to a cost/benefit analysis before implementation, and its impact monitored against expectation.

subsections.title: Free Health Checks
bullets: Benefits to insurer: early identification of impairments at a less severe (cheaper) stage; encouragement of healthier lifestyles.

For employer-sponsored business: reinforces employer care for staff wellbeing and enhances product value. For individually-purchased business: attracts health-conscious individuals, though may also attract hypochondriacs.
Can be used as a customer loyalty tool (e.g. offered at each annual PMI renewal).

Risk: free health checks may increase claims by identifying previously undetected illnesses — this must be weighed against earlier, cheaper treatment. Incentives like discounted gym memberships and wearable technology are also used in some markets.

subsections.title: Group Risks
bullets: For insurer covering a workforce at limited sites, there is a unique opportunity to influence the health risk environment through advising on workplace accident/infection risks and claims acceptance practices.
The insurer may employ a risk management expert to visit, assess, and collaborate (not dictate) with the employer on risk reduction — most cost-effective for larger schemes.

Employers co-operate because: sickness absence reduces productivity; work-related illness may create employer liability; many group schemes are experience rated (lower claims = lower cost); and paternalistic employer motivation.

Where a broker intermediates: the insurer has less direct access to the employer; the broker may be reluctant to impose unpopular measures. The insurer should help the broker appreciate the advantages to the employer of controlling risks.

subsections.title: Overview
bullets: Experience analysis may reveal problems addressable through changes to how distributors are paid, with the aim of cutting costs, increasing business, or improving persistency.
Potential issues identified by analysis of profitability by source: expenses too high; sales volumes too low; persistency too low; claims costs too high.

subsections.title: Reducing Remuneration
bullets: Possible approaches: freezing basic salaries or increasing below inflation; not promoting people; reducing non-contractual pay (bonuses, commission); cutting headcount or outsourcing to cheaper staff.

Problems: staff morale and performance deteriorate; trade union resistance and possible industrial action; loss of skilled staff and difficulty recruiting replacements; negative press reaction; long-established bonuses may have set legal precedent; contractual constraints on existing staff.

subsections.title: Rewarding Better Business
bullets: Pay higher commission for business that adds most to profitability. Where persistency improvement is the goal, shift the commission balance from initial to renewal commission.

Optimisation principle: increasing commission raises expenses but may boost volumes — the optimal commission level maximises overall insurer profit, considering the long-term profitability (allowing for lapse rates) of the product line and the competitive market commission rate.

subsections.title: Penalising Worse Experience
bullets: Tactics: reducing commission for distributors with significantly worse persistency; analysing distributors generating low proportions of non-standard business (may indicate non-disclosure); terminating arrangements with distributors providing no net positive contribution to profit.
Further tactics: increasing commission clawback levies for early lapsing policies; deferring initial commission until the policy has been in force for specified periods.

subsections.title: Improving Persistency (Non-Commission Tactics)
bullets: Loyalty bonuses or discounts for long-standing policyholders, or subtler measures such as free health checks at renewal.
Better sales training and supervised interviews to identify and attract the most profitable customer types; more prescribed client dialogue.
Attendance at sales conventions based on in-force business rather than new sales — a perk and incentive for maintaining a persistent book.

subsections.title: Changing Mix of Business
bullets: The insurer sets underwriting criteria for cases: accepted at standard rates / loaded premiums / with exclusions / declined. Loading is common for long-term business; exclusions are more common for PMI.

Underwriting information includes: application form (occupation, residence, travel, pastimes, personal/family medical history, lifestyle); supplementary medical/occupation questionnaires; GP report; Medical Examiner's report; medical test results.

Post-experience-analysis options: relax criteria to expand portfolio (only viable if sufficient margin; relaxing standards increases claims costs); or tighten criteria if risk premiums are not covering claims (improves average health of new entrants but increases underwriting costs).
Reinsurers have significant expertise in underwriting strategy and are well-placed to advise.

subsections.title: Improving Questions
bullets: Better/more detailed questions improve risk selection, but excess questions lose customer goodwill, create delays, and add admin cost — balance is needed.
Questions should be tailored to the sales channel (face-to-face selling supports more questions than off-the-page advertising).

Internet sales raise particular concerns: buyer is likely higher-risk and aware of health needs; easy price comparison attracts adverse selection; easier to test different answers before submitting; potentially easier to misrepresent to a computer than to a human interviewer.

subsections.title: Internal Control Levels
bullets: Tightening: require higher management authority to approve policies that were previously standard.

Loosening: allow point-of-sale acceptance for straightforward cases, with referral of complex cases to head office. A computerised decision system handles clear-cut responses; complex cases still go to underwriters. The quality of overall underwriting should not be compromised.

subsections.title: Automation
bullets: Computerised underwriting systems offer: speed, consistency, reduced human processing, and simpler admin linkage.

Disadvantages: does not utilise human underwriting experience; in health insurance, loadings may be generated from statistics that are not relevant or credible; experienced underwriters deal only with complex referrals and lose skill in routine cases.
Periodic sampling should compare automated decisions against what a human underwriter would decide.

Tele-underwriting (telephone-based supplemental questioning by a trained interviewer, often third-party) is an increasingly popular alternative, allowing additional information gathering without front-loading the application form.

subsections.title: The Claims Process
bullets: A sound claims procedure should be: smooth, efficient, and cost-effective in administration; compliant with policy conditions and exclusions; fast for valid claims with minimal inconvenience; sensitive and knowledgeable in handling (even for declined claims); aligned with customer expectations.
High customer satisfaction must be achieved without compromising underwriting standards, profitability, or reinsurer satisfaction. The process should be monitored regularly.

subsections.title: Training
bullets: Poor training is a common cause of claims deterioration and customer dissatisfaction. Other causes: high staff turnover, rapidly changing environment.
Remedies: enhanced initial and ongoing training; participation in industry forums and conferences (e.g. AMEI claims forums); review of claims staff remuneration and conditions to reduce turnover.

Examples of inexperienced assessor errors — IP: wrong disability definition used; fraudulent claims accepted; benefit paid when claimant can return to work; benefit exceeds salary cap. CI: claims accepted below policy trigger threshold; insufficient evidence for Terminal Illness or Total Permanent Disability. LTCI: claims accepted without ADL trigger; benefit level too high relative to ADL count. PMI: claims accepted for excluded/pre-existing conditions; chronic conditions paid beyond exclusion period; treatment fees exceed benefit limits. All products: greater potential for fraud, exaggerated claims, or missed non-disclosure.

subsections.title: More Accurate Information
bullets: Medical evidence: enhanced claims acceptance can involve additional questions on claim forms or supplementary tests by the claimant's GP, an independent doctor, or an insurer employee. Periodic checks (random or targeted at unusual behaviour) can correct ambiguities and feed improvements back into underwriting/claims.

Financial evidence: primarily relevant to IP claims; also CI and LTCI for high benefit amounts. IP policies contain benefit limitation clauses (usually a % of pre-disability salary) — earnings evidence is required to enforce these. Financial evidence also helps assess motivation to return to work; unwillingness to provide it can indicate fraud.

subsections.title: Involving All Parties in the Claims Process
bullets: Claims process design should involve product developers, pricers, and medical underwriters.

The liaison between underwriters and claims managers is critical to ensure: consistent application of exclusions at underwriting and claims stages; a claim is not paid for an illness that would have been excluded (or loaded) had it been disclosed at proposal.

subsections.title: Controlling Claims Costs (PMI)
bullets: PMI claims control has two dimensions: validity (is the claim covered under the policy?) and amount (is the quantum of the claim appropriate?).
Even where the policyholder is entitled to treatment, the insurer may negotiate provider charges (pre-event via schedules with hospital chains, or post-event by querying specific cost items).

Pre-authorisation requirements can also manage claims costs. The insurer may review clinical protocols with providers to confirm procedures are medically appropriate and necessary — keeping claims frequency and severity closer to the pricing basis.

subsections.title: Market Analysis
bullets: Compare actual returns against standard market benchmarks for the same period. If underperformance is in line with the market, that may be an acceptable explanation — though many will want to go further.

subsections.title: Comparing with Product Needs
bullets: Assess what investment return the product needed to achieve required profitability (the 'interest on reserves' pricing assumption).

Check whether liabilities are matched by corresponding assets — greater matching reduces the impact of investment underperformance. Matching is conceptually difficult for short-term health insurance; more formally attempted for long-term products, especially LTCI.
If the shortfall cannot be resolved by matching, investment return assumptions in the pricing basis may need revision, leading to premium rate review.

subsections.title: Selecting Investment Managers
bullets: A change of investment manager may improve returns. For health and care insurance, investment return is a relatively minor profit driver; any investment change is considered in the context of the wider company investment strategy.

subsections.title: Changing Investment Strategy
bullets: Consider alternative asset classes bearing in mind: security, term, currency, type, liquidity, and risk. Higher-risk strategies require the actuary to justify the additional return against the risk of non-achievement.

Insurers with large free assets can support more volatile investment strategies. Factors that reduce free assets include: large volumes of new business (new business strain); shareholder distributions; with-profits bonus distributions; adverse claims/investment experience; changes to reinsurance strategy (e.g. reducing financial reinsurance); strengthening of reserving basis.

subsections.title: Requirements and Considerations
bullets: From the actuary's perspective, the required IT service should: capture and retain policy data; analyse data and import from other sources as needed; interface with the insurer's existing IT infrastructure; bear a cost supportable by product IT expense margins; be flexible to accommodate anticipated future changes.
Key considerations: in-house vs external; mainframe vs PC-server; improved monitoring/pricing software; time and expense to implement changes, and prioritisation.
There is an increasing trend towards IT outsourcing, particularly advantageous for newer/rapidly expanding insurers not constrained by legacy system compatibility.

sections.title: Changing Office Personnel and Competence Balance

subsections.title: Recruiting or Promoting
bullets: Analysis may reveal decisions being made by staff who are too senior (unnecessarily expensive) where junior staff could adequately decide, or decisions requiring more experienced staff than currently available.

Common examples arise in medical underwriting and claims control. Certain processes may benefit from specialist expertise (e.g. clinical expertise for specialist health products; local market knowledge for overseas operations).

subsections.title: Training
bullets: Existing staff can be trained for more expert roles — less costly than recruitment if urgency is not critical. External companies often provide this training.

subsections.title: External Expertise
bullets: External sources (consultants, reinsurers) provide one-off specialist support.
The choice between consultant and reinsurer depends on: explicit vs implicit cost; expertise and value offered.
The actuary should be able to identify situations where each might be used and the criteria for choosing between them.

22.summary:
Introducing new products
• May arise from saturated market for existing products or too few customers buying existing products for them to make financial sense.

Abandoning a product
• Consider the implications of abandoning a product, eg: can create negative customer sentiment and ‘brand damage’; the portfolio may reduce, leaving less data for analysis and a deterioration in experience due to selective lapsing; there will be some reinsurer reaction, that will depend on the nature of the relationship with the insurer and the expected length of the treaty.
• Impacts more significant on short-term or renewable products, existing long-term contracts must continue cover to the end of the term.

Changing policy conditions
• Contract may be changed to offer a better benefit package (eg to bring it in line with the rest of the market) or to improve profitability (eg by increasing premiums on reviewable contracts).
• The legality of the policy change must be checked, terms cannot be worsened and requirements to treat customers fairly must be met. Policyholders’ reasonable expectations under reviewable contracts can be difficult to gauge. Policyholders should be reminded of the possibility of change. The reinsurer should be involved.
• A step out-of-line with other insurers may be viewed with suspicion. Industry bodies can help to enable change for the whole market.
• Changing existing policies for consistency with new business helps reduce administrative complexity.
• The impact of any change (eg on profitability or future solvency) should be modelled using revised assumptions. A stochastic approach may be particularly appropriate for health insurance.

Improving the risk environment
• Methods of improving the risk environment to reduce claims include: offering free health checks; managing group risks, eg working with the employer to reduce the potential for accident and disease in the workplace.
• The insurer will have less direct influence on an employer where there is a broker involved.

Changing distributor remuneration structures
• Remuneration may be reviewed to cut costs or increase new business sales. Salaries could be reduced (eg in real terms) or frozen. Commission could be increased on more profitable or persistent business.
• Persistency may also be improved by ‘loyalty’ bonuses or discounts, better sales procedures and rewarding sales staff based on persistency and risk measures.

Changing medical underwriting
• Changing underwriting standards will change the mix of business by those: accepted at ordinary rates, loaded premiums or exclusions and declined.
• Consider the number and level of detail of the questions asked, the standards of underwriting (eg the level of underwriter dealing with each case) and point-of-sales systems used to accept cases automatically.
• A computerised process to accept all but the most complex applications would be quick, consistent, cheaper and tie in with admin systems. It will not make use of human experience and the data used to make decisions may be irrelevant, out-of-date or non-credible. Regular monitoring would be required.

Changing claims management
• Should achieve a high level of customer satisfaction without compromising underwriting standards or profitability.
• Could be improved by: increasing training and sharing experience; gathering more, or more accurate, evidence; involving all parties in the claim process (eg underwriters and policy developers); checking the quality of information gathered.

Changing investment policy or managers
• If the investment performance has not met expectations, could consider: performance against the rest of the market; the return required to achieve profitability criteria, the extent of matching to liabilities and whether premium rates need to be reviewed; changing the investment manager; changing the investment strategy.

Changing IT structures
• Requirements include to capture, retain and analyse the data, link with existing systems and flexibility to meet future changes.
• Consider the expenses of implementing changes and maintaining the system.

Changing office personnel and competence balance
• Addressed concerns about whether appropriate staff members are making decisions by recruiting or restructuring to ensure the right level of expertise is utilised in making decisions, training existing staff or making increased use of external expertise (consultant or reinsurer).

5.title: General Business Environment (1)
5.summary:
The healthcare marketplace

The key drivers of demand are:
• the increasing level of wealth and sophistication
• demographic trends (people living longer, especially in disability or with chronic conditions)
• economic conditions (reducing demand for savings and increasing demand for protection)
• the level and form of state health care provision
• employer provided benefits
• technology trends.

Customer perception of the health insurance industry is influenced by:
• public understanding of health and care products (particularly an issue in developing markets)
• tax incentives
• adverse media coverage (for example due to corporate failures, mis-selling or compliance breaches).

Other factors that will affect the health and care insurance market are:
• competition in the market – products are often niche leading to few providers
• overseas State systems and insurance solutions – helps to identify opportunities in other markets, careful analysis and market testing should be carried out before development
• technology – eg new business acquisitions, underwriting and claims management, price comparison sites
• data science – offers advantages around new product design, improved services and increased inclusivity for customers. Risks around consent, data security, compatibility with legacy systems, staff skills and ethical issues around the use of data, eg to influence customer behaviour. Regulation in this area is evolving
• climate change – wide-ranging impact on health and mortality, physical assets and financial markets
• pandemics – significant threat to health and care insurers.

Methods of sale
• Each channel attracts clients with particular demographic profiles, and need different contract designs and pricing.
• Sales methods may be regulated, for example: types of products sold; how advice provided / paid for; charge caps; requirements around who can distribute certain products; restrictions on competition; how mis-selling is dealt with.

Mis-selling
• Could lead to litigation, bad publicity, complaints and claim decisions made against the insurer.
• Technology is having an impact on reducing mis-selling, eg the growth of insurtech start-ups that find efficiencies in claims and policy management. Not so easy to get technology improvements in developing markets.

The role of the State in healthcare provision
• Insurers try to complement the role of the State.
• Means testing certain State benefits creates a need for health insurance. Means testing may be driven by demographic change, commitment to a low tax environment and other competing demands on public expenditure.
• The future role of the State in healthcare provision is crucial to an insurer’s strategy. Among the indicators are: IP insurance – levels and public awareness of State benefits, preventative and rehabilitation measures, changes to State benefits; LTCI – means-testing thresholds and how LTC will be funded; PMI – government commitment to State healthcare funding and tax culture; all – demographic changes.

The role of employers in provision of health and care benefits

IP insurance and PMI mainly sold on a group basis. The attitude of employers to providing such benefits is key to the insurer’s future revenue streams, influenced by:
• value of benefits to the employer (eg a perk, or enabling early return to work)
• tax efficiency
• cost relative to individual purchase (group usually cheaper).

6.title: General Business Environment (2)
6.summary:
Medical underwriting and pre-existing condition clauses
• Used to obtain accurate risk classification so correct premium charged.
• Front-end underwriting – uses information from proposal form, medical report, medical examination / tests and supplementary questionnaires. Insured knows what events are / are not covered by the policy, underwriting decision may take some time.
• Back-end underwriting – provides immediate cover without formal underwriting. Underwriting performed at claims stage, conditions that were / are related to those pre-existing at application are excluded. May lead to uncertainty over what is covered. Can use ‘moratorium’ approach to back-end underwriting for PMI.
• IP insurance underwriting now involves considering psychosocial factors (how people behave towards illness and medical history). Group IP cover uses: ‘once only’ underwriting; tele-interviewing; face-to-face underwriting; company medical exam data.

Genetics
• Advances in genetic testing may lead to: anti-selection (unless all relevant information is declared); unaffordable premiums, leading to non-disclosure of relevant information; need for new product designs, revised pricing, updated underwriting procedures or withdrawal from market.
• Increased availability and cheapness of tests may increase the above impacts.
• May be regulatory restrictions around requiring genetic tests to be taken, or using the results of such tests as part of the underwriting process.

Use of counterparties
• Insurer must assess, quantify and mitigate credit and counterparty risks. Managed by carefully worded service level agreement and regular monitoring.
• Main counterparties for a health insurer include: third party outsourcers, involved in an increasing number of areas; brokers, who may hold back premiums due to the insurer (brokers’ balances); hospitals, consultants and other medical providers; independent investigators and assessors, eg on IP claims.

External influences
• Populations in the developed world are: getting older, placing pressure on State resources; living longer, changing claim rates; having fewer children, increasing individuals’ wealth, placing pressure on the State.
• Impact of medical advances varies by product (eg IP reduces incidence rates but prolongs survival, PMI increases incidence rates and claim costs).
• Greater availability of some surgical procedures and simpler diagnosis increases the scope for anti-selection (eg windfall payments) on CI insurance. Emergence of new diseases puts pressure on CI insurance providers to include these and can lead to increased claims on other health products.
• Insurers should consider economic factors, including: business optimism, eg on new business and renewals (particularly for groups); employment security, eg claims (IP insurance and PMI), use of IP insurance as an early retirement vehicle, new business and renewals.
• It should also allow for: political influences; social influences; pandemics; climate change – physical, transition and liability risks; other environment factors, such as air pollution; mental health conditions.

Medical conditions
• Main conditions affecting mortality and morbidity rates change over time.
• Life expectancy has improved, but people can expect to spend more years in ill health (impacting LTCI providers). Causes vary significantly by region.
• Five specific conditions affecting mortality rates are: heart disease – key risk factors are smoking, obesity and high blood pressure; cancer – recent improvements in survival rates will affect CI insurers, giving rise to more and potentially earlier claims; stroke; respiratory disease – the main risk factor is smoking but can also be work-related; liver disease – major causes are obesity, undiagnosed infection and harmful drinking, all of which are preventable.

7.title: Product Design and Pricing (1)
7.summary:
Income protection insurance

Claim definitions:
• Definitions can: be occupation based (eg own occupation, any occupation); be based on an abilities test (eg ADWs); change after a specified claim duration.
• Maybe maximum payment period for some causes (eg mental illness).
• Claim definition for housepersons can be: Category 1: dependent on ADLs; Category 2: inability to perform normal household duties.
• Exclusions (by cause of illness) may be applied.
• Expiry age normally linked to retirement.

Claims management:
• benefit limitation
• proportionate benefit
• rehabilitation / partial benefit
• telephone helplines.

Critical illness insurance

Claim definitions:
• Benefit paid on diagnosis of specific illness or undergoing specific treatment.
• Lump sum gives rise to possible windfall opportunities; some policies offer severity-based payments.
• Medical advancements accelerate claim payment and increase windfall claims.
• Medical advancements may reduce need for certain treatments, reducing claims.
• Policy wordings must follow medical advancements; may have reviewable terms.

Claims management:
• assessment period
• waiting period
• telephone helplines.

Long-term care insurance – immediate needs

Claim definitions:
• Protect individuals from their uncertain survival duration.
• Benefit escalation may be: pre-agreed with care home(s); fixed rate pa; linked to RPI.

Claims management:
• assessment period
• telephone helplines.

Long-term care insurance – pre-funded

Claim definitions:
• Claims definition – may be single or multiple events trigger. Order of events may matter.
• Determined by ADLs.
• Payments for lifetime, or may have maximum duration.
• Escalation as for immediate needs.

Claims management:
• assessment period
• telephone helplines.

Private medical insurance

Claim conditions:
• Claim definitions relate to the treatments covered.
• Policy wording can be changed annually.
• Exclusions are important.

Claims management:
• excess or deductible
• coinsurance
• internal limits
• no claims discount
• pre-authorisation
• telephone helplines.

Health cash plans

Claim definitions:
• Pre-specified cash sum paid on certain medical events.

Claims management:
• excess or deductible
• coinsurance
• internal limits
• telephone helplines.

Consumer demand and competition

On launching, redesigning or re-pricing a product consider:
• customer demand and acceptable price levels
• target market and size of market
• corporate image
• experience of existing providers and position in market (niche or existing product).

Distribution
• Needs to assess the willingness of intermediaries to sell the product. Only specialist intermediaries will sell some products.
• Undertake research to establish whether the new product is going to be sold in substitution for an existing product.

Loss leaders
• A new product may need to be sold at a discounted price to achieve market acceptance (or to cross-sell other products to these customers).
• Finances of the company must be reviewed to ensure sufficient cashflow to offset early losses and that these can be recouped in the future. The statutory responsibility to treat customers fairly may prevent the latter.

Cross-selling
• Consider selling to existing customers to achieve savings (including on admin costs).
• Products should be appealing to existing customers if they have been satisfied so far. Overlaps in benefits can be removed.

Underwriting
• Guidelines should be set by the actuary as part of product design to meet pricing objectives. Underwriting decisions need to be consistent over time.
• Any legal restrictions on underwriting should be noted.

8.title: Product Design and Pricing (2)
8.summary:
Cost of capital
• Regulation imposes significant reserving and statutory capital requirements that lead to capital strain at policy issue. Capital requirements can be assessed using model offices with sensitivity tests.
• Product design and reinsurance can be used to minimise capital requirements.
• Cost of capital is the loss of return on shareholders’ investments resulting from holding reserves. It should be allowed for in the pricing models, taking into account: competing demands on the use of capital; the returns required by the different capital providers; the returns being obtained from other products or ventures.
• If the cost of capital is ignored in pricing, a subsidy must be provided from other business.
• Increasingly companies are adopting a market-consistent approach to product pricing, more consistent with embedded values, but can lead to greater volatility in the pricing.

Risks and risk management
• Need to consider risks linked to the product design and pricing process: product substitution; too many sales (capital problems); sales failure; expense overruns; product design failure; product costing failure (parameter error).
• Estimated claim rates and volatility are influenced by the claims management system. It should deal with all claims administration and payments, and be fully integrated where there are large claim numbers (eg PMI). It can be off-the-shelf or belong to a third party.
• Other claims control factors include pre-authorisation of PMI claims, rehabilitation of IP insurance claimants and counselling of IP insurance claimants and families of LTCI claimants.
• Customers need to be made ‘risk aware’ to achieve marketing success.
• A balance is needed between the most relevant data for pricing and the most credible. The actuary should take appropriate action to minimise the impact when statistics are scarce.

Reinsurance
• Product design may be able to take advantage of tax and capital arbitrage opportunities available through reinsurance.
• Reinsurers may also provide technical assistance (eg pricing data, market surveys, product design and wording guidance).

Investment return assumption
• Depends on the chosen investment policy, which should be consistent with the proposed liability structure.
• The extent of mis-matching depends on the company’s ‘risk appetite’, free assets, rate of portfolio growth and need for liquidity.

Inflation assumptions
• Assumptions regarding the inflation of claims, premiums, administration expenses and claim expenses should be set consistently.

The renewal process and options

Renewability
• CI, IP insurances and LTCI are guaranteed renewable.
• PMI and Cash Plans are not normally guaranteed renewable. Renewal terms may be identical to the terms for new entrants or reflect past experience (eg experience rating or NCD).

Reviewability
• Uncertainty in future experience can be reflected in changes to benefits and/or premiums. Premiums can be reviewed to reflect the claims experience of the whole portfolio. Upward premium reviews can encourage selective lapsing.
• Lapse and re-entry risk exists for reviewable contracts. Regulators may issue guidance to help ensure reviewable contract design is fair to customers.

Options
• Benefit increases can be incorporated in the original contract (and premiums may or may not also increase) or the increase is costed as it occurs. Both versions will be without further evidence of health.
• Guarantees require significant margins in pricing basis and in reserves. High levels of uncertainty have led to reviewable contracts becoming more common.

Converting the form of benefits
• Lump sum benefits can be converted into income via a purchased life annuity (PLA). Impaired life annuity rates may be offered.
• Converting income benefits (eg from IP insurance or LTCI) into capital is difficult. The reason for the request and medical implications need to be considered.

Regulatory requirements
• Product design should fit into the regulatory environment considering: authorisation to write each class of business; pricing restrictions; the potential for future pricing restrictions; establishment of appropriate sales routines and disclosure; capital requirements.

Environmental, social and governance (ESG)
• There is increasing integration of ESG considerations in investment practices due to: risk and return; the public interest; ethical reasons; consumer pressure.

Other considerations
• Barriers for consumers – insurers should take care to remove any barriers that are preventing customers accessing their services.
• Administration – systems need to be ready and tested prior to product launch. Key staff must be recruited and trained.
• Expenses – all the expenses that are associated with a product need to be allowed for in pricing.
• Lapses – important factors in determining lapse rates include: distribution methods and after-sales service; economic confidence; ongoing competitiveness of the product. Competitive pressures have caused lapse and re-entry problems for insurers.

9.title: Taxation Environment
9.summary:
Overview
• Tax can be based on: profits; investment income less expenses; premium income.
• Other taxes (such as stamp duty or employment related taxes) may also be payable.
• Policyholders (or their dependents) may be taxed on benefits.
• The taxation environment can change over time and may impact: the amount and timing of tax payable by an insurer; the attractiveness of insurance products (and compared to equivalent products offered by other institutions); the design of insurance products; policyholders buying habits.

The UK tax environment

Long-term business
• Long-term protection business is taxed as PHI business provided: benefits are payable on accident or sickness as defined in the contract; the term is at least five years (or to retirement if less); the contract is (broadly) not cancellable by the insurer.
• The majority of CI insurance, IP insurance and LTCI are classed as long-term business.
• Post January 2013, insurers pay corporation tax on pre-tax trading profits of long-term business. The accounts should be prepared in accordance with relevant accounting rules.
• Before January 2013, some long-term protection business was taxed on an I-E basis.

Short-term business
• Insurers pay corporation tax on tradable profits for short-term business (such as PMI). This is taxable profits adjusted for: investment return (excluding dividend income) – taxed on a fair value basis; the underwriting result and technical provision; other items (depreciation / disallowable entertaining); overseas / foreign profit.
• The underwriting result is premiums less claims and expenses less increase in technical provisions.
• The technical provisions are calculated on an accounting basis and an actuarial opinion is required to confirm the amount is ‘not excessive’. Technical provisions can include: reported claims provisions with case estimates or statistical provisions; provision for unearned premiums on the most appropriate basis; provision for unexpired risk reserve on a statistical basis; provisions for claims handing expenses.
• Tax losses may be: carried back and set against the previous year’s profits; carried forward and set against future trading profits; surrendered to other companies in the same UK tax group and used to offset the taxable profits of those companies.
• For a mutual insurer writing short-term insurance, underwriting losses / profits from mutual trading are exempt from tax. Investment income is taxed independently. If they make an underwriting loss, it cannot be offset against investment profits.

Taxation of policyholders
• Tax of policyholders may depend on the type of business, who receives the benefit and who has paid the premiums.

UK policyholder taxation
• CI insurance: individual policies – no tax relief on premiums, benefits of stand-alone policies are tax free; group policies – if the employer pays the premiums it is usually deductible as a business expense for the employer, the employee would be taxed on the premium as a ‘benefit in kind’.
• IP insurance: individual policies – no tax relief on premiums, benefits are tax-free if premiums are paid by the life insured; group policies – if premiums are paid by the employer for the employee classified as business expense for the employer and qualify for tax relief, if premiums are paid by the employee no tax relief. If benefits are paid to the employer, taxed as a trading receipt, if they are then passed to the employee as a salary income tax will be payable.
• LTCI – varies depending on whether pre-funded or immediate needs product.
• PMI: no tax on claims; premiums – insurance premium tax is payable, if paid by employer paid out of pre-tax income (ie available for tax relief) but if paid by policyholders paid from post-tax income.

10.title: Approaches to Regulation
10.summary:
Regulatory objectives
• Policyholder protection and financial stability.
• Changes driven by the economic environment, harmonization across jurisdictions and advances in risk management.
• Insurers need to comply with local regulations and any supra-national regulators.

Regulatory responsibilities

Regulators normally produce rulebooks setting out rules / principles. Additional guidance may also be provided. Regulation may cover:
• Authorisation – may be required for individual jurisdictions or granted as part of a wider economic area.
• Business standards – affect day-to-day business, may cover senior management systems and controls (eg compliance, risk management, outsourcing, regulatory processes) and market conduct (requirements to act honestly, fairly and professionally eg around communication with clients, information disclosure to customers, product information, complaints)
• Supervisory reporting – specify information and frequency of reporting, may include balance sheet, revenue account, capital requirements (and assumptions / model), details of new business, forward looking analysis, stress / scenario testing
• Monitoring – regular assessment visits, review of reports discussed at governance meetings, review of regulatory reports
• Valuation and capital requirements – rules / principles around the valuation of assets and liabilities, assumptions, reinsurance and capital requirements
• Fit and proper person – specify rules by which those in senior / statutory actuarial roles need to operate, eg Chief Actuary may be responsible for ensuring technical provisions, appropriate methodology / assumptions etc, requirements on the insurer to keep the actuary informed, pay due regard to their advice and provide them with adequate resources
• Actions taken by regulators – may be able to require insurer to remedy situation, impose fines, require reinsurance to be purchased, increase capital requirements etc, some groups may be viewed as too large to fail (global systematically important insurers, G-SIIs) are subject to enhanced supervision
• Transfer of surplus – regulations around how much an insurer can transfer out of insurance fund (eg to pay dividends), may require surplus to be held in liquid assets
• Transfer of liabilities between insurers – may require Court and regulatory approval, independent expert may review impact on policyholders (in terms of benefits and TCF), may require scheme to be publicised to policyholder / shareholders.

Other legislation and guidance

Professional guidance

The IFoA has issued the following Actuarial Profession Standards:
• APS L1 (‘Duties and responsibilities of life assurance actuaries’)
• APS L2 (‘The Financial Services and Markets Act 2000 (Communications by Actuaries) Regulations 2003’)
• APS X1 – Applying Standards to Actuarial Work
• APS X2 – Review of Actuarial Work
• APS X3 – The Actuary as an Expert in Legal Proceedings
• APS QA1 – Quality Assurance Scheme for Organisations.

Consumer legislation – May be specific legislation around what is a fair contract to avoid a significant imbalance.

Equality legislation – Discrimination based on factors such as age, disability, gender and pregnancy may be unlawful. Can cause issues when estimating the mix of business.

Climate change – Regulators are working on regulations that aim to limit the impact of climate change on financial systems.

Regulatory approach by jurisdiction

United Kingdom
• Prudential Regulation Authority (PRA) – responsible for the prudential regulation of insurers to provide policyholder protection and promote resilience to failure (but not prevent failure).
• Financial Conduct Authority (FCA) – responsible for market conduct within the insurance industry.

United States
• State regulation co-ordinated by the National Association of Insurance Commissioners (NAIC), sets out model laws to encourage uniformity of reporting, valuation and capital requirements across states.
• At a federal level: Federal Insurance Office – monitors all aspects of the insurance sectors, advises on important international and national insurance matters; Federal Reserve Board – consolidated oversight of systematically important insurers.

China
• China Banking and Insurance Regulatory Commission – covers establishment and ongoing business activities of banking and insurers, including enforcement against regulatory violations. Implemented changes around capital requirements, risk management and disclosure transparency through C-ROSS framework.

Australia
• Australian Prudential Regulation Authority – takes a risk-based approach to help identify areas of greatest risk to target in a cost effective manner. Developed Life and General Insurance Capital standards aligned to Solvency II in the EU.

South Africa
• Regulation split between the Prudential Authority and the Financial Sector Conduct Authority.
• The Solvency Assessment Management framework is a risk-based approach to prudential regulation.

11.title: Solvency II (1)
11.summary:
Overview
• The Solvency II framework comprises three pillars: Pillar 1: Quantitative requirements; Pillar 2: Qualitative requirements and supervisory review; Pillar 3: Reporting, disclosure and market discipline.

Pillar 2
• The Board has overall responsibility for ongoing compliance with Solvency II.
• All insurance companies required to have a: risk management function; actuarial function; compliance function; internal audit function.
• Insurers must carry out an Own Risk and Solvency Assessment (ORSA), requires: identification of all risks (including qualitative risks) and related risk management processes and controls; quantification of ability to continue to meet MCR and SCR, allowing for new business; considered for strategic decisions.

Pillar 3
• Results of the solvency calculation, ORSA and risk management processes disclosed to the supervisor in the: Regular Supervisory Report (RSR), submitted annually; Quantitative Reporting Templates (QRT), submitted annually (with a subset quarterly).
• Public Solvency and Financial Condition Report (SFCR) produced annually.

Group reporting
• Each insurance group must cover its overall group SCR and group solvency floor. Each insurance subsidiary needs to cover its own SCR and MCR.
• Group supervision normally carried out at the top level company within the EEA. Additional rules apply to subsidiaries and parents located in a ‘third country’, ie non-EEA, depending on whether the country is equivalent or not.

Impact on business culture and strategy
• Engagement with Solvency II important throughout the business, up to senior management and Board level.
• Solvency II affects almost all activities, including: risk management and capital allocation; product mix and product design; asset mix; corporate structure; management information; external disclosures.

12.title: Solvency II (2)
12.summary:
Valuation of assets
• Valued using a market-consistent approach, ie at market value where available and mark-to-model where not.
• Recoveries expected from reinsurance shown as an asset on the balance sheet.

Valuation of technical provisions
• Based on a market-consistent approach, represent the amount the insurer would pay to transfer its obligations immediately to another insurer.
• Best estimate liability (BEL) and a risk margin: BEL is the present value of expected future cashflows, discounted using a ‘risk-free’ yield curve (ie term-dependent rates); Discount rate may be increased by a matching adjustment or a volatility adjustment if prescribed conditions met; Risk margin is intended to increase the technical provision to the amount that would have to be paid to another insurer for them to take on the BEL. Determined using the ‘cost of capital’ method.
• For unit-linked, the unit and non-unit components must be unbundled.

SCR
• Value at Risk measure based on a 99.5% confidence level of the variation over one year of the amount of ‘basic own funds’.
• Can be calculated using either standard formula or internal model (or a mixture).

Standard formula
• The Basic SCR (BSCR) is calculated by considering different modules of risks: market (interest rate, equity, property, credit spread, currency and concentration); counterparty default; insurance (separately for life, health and non-life business); intangible assets.
• The health insurance risk module comprised of SLT health, non-SLT health and catastrophe.
• SCR calculated separately for each risk module, results are aggregated using a specified correlation matrix to give the BSCR.
• The overall SCR obtained by adding allowances for the operational risk module and the loss absorbing capacity of technical provisions and deferred taxes.

Internal models
• Internal model must pass the following tests to gain approval: use test; statistical quality standards; calibration standards; profit and loss attribution; validation standards; documentation standards.

MCR
• Defined as a simple factor-based linear formula targeted at a Value-at-Risk measure over one year with 85% confidence.
• Floor of 25% and cap of 45% of the SCR.

Supervisory intervention levels
• Increasingly severe supervisory authority actions taken as a company’s eligible capital falls below the SCR (first point of intervention) and approaches the MCR.
• MCR is the ultimate point of supervisory intervention, below which the company would lose its authorisation.

Quality of capital resources
• ‘Own funds’ refers to assets in excess of technical provisions, plus it includes any subordinated liabilities, split into basic and ancillary own funds, which are then tiered based on specific criteria.
• Restrictions placed on the quality of capital used to cover the MCR and SCR.

Data quality
• EIOPA has issued advice on ‘Standards for Data Quality’, highlights the importance of having good quality data for the valuation of technical provisions.

13.title: Other Approaches to Solvency Regulation
13.summary:
United Kingdom
• Currently follows the Solvency II regulations of the EU, unclear how this will change following the UK’s exit from the EU.

USA
• National system of state-based regulation. National Association of Insurance Commissioners (NAIC) assists regulators to protect consumers and maintain financial stability.
• Insurers are required to maintain prudent reserves, capital and surplus at all times.
• Risk-based capital (RBC) system – uses a standard formula to determine capital requirements, derived from annual statement, considers size and risk profile of the insurer. Regulators can take preventative and corrective measures where the RBS result is considered too low, it is not the full amount of capital an insurer would need to hold. Conservative reserves are also required.
• Solvency Modernisation Initiative (SMI) – review started in 2008 following financial crash.
• Principle-Based Reserving (PBR) – replaced formula-driven approaches, insurers hold the highest of prescribed net premiums reserve, single economic scenario deterministic reserve and a stochastic reserve.
• ORSA – insurers are required to analyse all reasonably foreseeable and relevant material risks that might impact its ability to meet policyholder obligations.
• Key differences to Solvency II are: approach to regulation; capital – RBC is a ‘weaker’ level of capital requirement than SCR under Solvency II; statutory reserving – US approach historically formula-based with margins in assumptions for prudence, changing going forward following PBR, Solvency II requires market-consistent estimate of liabilities plus an explicit risk margin.

China
• C-ROSS framework has three tiers: Pillar I – quantitative capital requirements including risk factors applied to premiums and reserves for risks such as insurance, market and credit; Pillar II – qualitative capital requirements composed of a rating to determine the level of any regulatory intervention, explicit assessment of insurer’s risk management processes (ORSA equivalent) and a series of liquidity risk indicators; Pillar III – risk disclosure and transparency.
• Key differences to Solvency II are: Asset valuation – accounting value for C-ROSS, mark to market for Solvency II; Discount rate – C-ROSS similar to Chinese GAAP liability valuation, Solvency II uses risk-free curve; Own solvency risk assessment – C-ROSS doesn’t allow internal models.

Australia
• Life and General Insurance Capital – intended to increase risk sensitivity and provide consistency, strong parallels to Solvency II (has provisional equivalence): required to hold capital to 99.5% VaR over 1 year, calculated using standard method or approved internal model; regulator (APRA) can apply supervisory adjustment if capital considered inappropriate; required to produce an individual capital adequacy assessment report (similar to ORSA).

South Africa
• Solvency Assessment and Management framework – risk-based solvency framework with 3 pillars, based on Solvency II adapted for South African market: Pillar 1 – quantitative regulatory requirements, capital requirements reflect 1-in-200 year loss event, can use standardised formula for approved internal model; Pillar 2 – qualitative requirements and rules on supervision; Pillar 3 – regulatory reporting and public disclosure.

Asia / Pacific
• Range of approaches taken from minimum levels of capital (eg Myanmar) to formula based approaches (eg India), risk-based capital (eg Indonesia) and economic capital and ORSA approaches (eg Australia and China).

Latin America
• Mexico adopted Solvency II-like framework.
• Brazil granted provisional equivalence.
• Chile developing risk-based capital approach modelled on Solvency II.
• Brazil and Chile both have ORSA equivalents.

14.title: Profit Reporting
14.summary:
IFRS
• The IFRS standards (produced by the IASB) are the most widely adopted accounting standards in the world. EU regulations require all listed companies to use them when preparing their consolidated financial statements.
• IFRS standards of particular interest to health and care insurers are: IFRS 17 Insurance Contracts; IFRS 9 Financial Instruments; IFRS 7 Financial Instruments: Disclosures.
• Principles-based, main principles are fair representation, going concern, accruals based, comparability, compliance, reporting period, understandability and offsetting.

IFRS 17 Insurance Contracts
• IFRS 17 took effect from 1 January 2023. Addresses criticisms of previous regime (IFRS 4).
• General Measurement Model (GMM) – has four building blocks: the fulfilment cashflows; the time value of money; the risk adjustment (RA); the contractual service margin (CSM)
• Variable Fee Approach (VFA) – may be used for contracts in which the cashflows vary in line with the value of a pool of assets (eg unit-linked, with-profits).

Accounting for financial instruments

Currently contained in IFRS 9.

US GAAP
• Set of generally accepted accounting principles adopted by the US Securities and Exchange Commission (‘SEC’). Developed by many different organisations with the primary body of guidance for the health and care insurance actuarial and accounting work being produced by the Financial Accounting Standards Board (‘FASB’).
• Principles-based, with some differences from and some similarities to IFRS.
• The main underlying principles are: fair presentation; going concern; compliance; historical cost; revenue recognition; matching; full disclosure; objectivity; consistency; offsetting.

Supplementary reporting
• Embedded value (EV) techniques are intended to measure a realistic, risk-adjusted, valuation of shareholder cashflows arising from in-force business and net assets. Profit then defined as the change in embedded value plus the profit transfer.
• The usefulness of supplementary EV reporting impaired by a lack of consistency and transparency in the methods and assumptions adopted by different insurance companies. Led to the development of the European Embedded Value (EEV) principles and Market Consistent Embedded Value (MCEV) principles.
• Under Solvency II, the basic liability (BEL) is determined on a best estimate basis and so there are no prudential margins to be released in the EV PVIF.
• May still be a PVIF component in relation to: profits expected beyond the Solvency II contract boundaries; any difference between the best estimate investment returns assumed in the EV projection basis and the discount rates used in the BEL (including any matching or volatility adjustment); the release of the risk margin, after allowing for the cost of holding it.
• Some health and care insurers in jurisdictions that have implemented Solvency II are no longer reporting on EV.

15.title: Professional Standards and Guidance
15.summary:
Principles of professional conduct
• Actuaries are expected to comply with any relevant professional standards or guidance set by the professional body to which they belong. Standards evolve over time to reflect the market, regulations and international actuarial standards.
• Typically principles-based and include codes of conduct and disciplinary procedures.

Professional Standards Framework – UK
• The Actuaries’ Code – sets out 6 core principles: integrity, competence and care, impartiality, compliance, speaking up and communication.
• Actuarial Profession Standards (APSs) – the IFoA has issued the following APSs: APS L1: Duties and Responsibilities of Life Assurance Actuaries; APS L2: The Financial Services and Markets Act 2000 (Communications by Actuaries) Regulations 2003; APS X1: Applying Standards to Actuarial Work; APS X2: Review of Actuarial Work; APS X3: The Actuary as an Expert in Legal Proceedings; APS G1: The Chief Actuary in Non-Life Insurance; APS QA1: Quality Assurance Scheme for Organisations.
• Guidance Notes – specific mandatory requirements members are expected to comply with.
• Other non-mandatory resource material – issued by the IFoA, include: Speaking up – a guide for actuaries; Speaking up – a guide for employers of actuaries; Conflicts of interest – a guide for actuaries; Ethical data science.
• Technical Actuarial Standards – principles-based and issued by the FRC, of relevance to Subject SA1 are: TAS 100 (Generic TAS): Principles for Actuarial Work; TAS 200: Insurance. The FRC has also developed a ‘Framework for FRC Technical Actuarial Standards’ describing the authority, scope and application of all TASs, and a glossary of terms.

International Actuarial Professional Standards
• Consistency in the approach to setting professional standards is influenced by the International Actuarial Association (IAA) and the Actuarial Association of Europe (AAE): IAA – worldwide association of professional actuarial bodies, develops model International Standards of Actuarial Practice (ISAPs), encourages member associations to adopt or have standards that are consistent with these; AAE – fulfils a similar role within Europe, also provides advice and opinions to organisations within the EU, develops European Standards of Actuarial Practice (ESAPs) similarly to ISAPs, has a ‘Code of Professional Conduct’ and requires member associations to have a code that reflects these requirements.

16.title: Capital Management
16.summary:
Capital requirements
• The three main measures of required capital are: economic capital – required to support the ongoing business strategy, reflecting the risk profile and risk appetite of the company; regulatory capital – required to satisfy the regulatory rules; rating agency capital – required to meet the standards of rating agencies.
• Insurers require capital as: risk capital – to cover the day-to-day risks; working capital – to support the ongoing business strategy, such as to fund new business, overheads and development costs of organic expansion (eg such as computer hardware and software, product development, head office and branch premises, and overseas initiatives), to acquire other companies or blocks of business.

Reasons for projecting solvency
• To understand and monitor a company’s risk profile over time.
• To allow the impact of key business decisions to be assessed, including determining supportable new business volumes.
• To estimate the pattern of capital releases to shareholders.
• To meet any statutory requirements, eg as part of the ORSA and to determine the risk margin under Solvency II.
• To help with successful risk measurement and risk management.

Available capital
• A proprietary’s available capital will be affected by: initial capital and any subsequent capital injections; profits or losses being retained within the company; shareholder dividends.
• The available capital for a mutual typically from: initial (and very occasionally subsequent) capital injections; past under-distribution of surplus in respect of with-profits policies.
• Realistic capital is the best measure of available capital from a policyholder and shareholder perspective.

Assessing ongoing solvency
• Projections of the ongoing solvency position are used to assess future capital requirements.
• Economic capital – Realistic capital can be determined using a number of scenarios, using stochastic modelling or stress tests. The time horizon considered could be a one-year period or the full run-off of the in-force business.
• Regulatory capital – Most likely risk sensitive and targeting a different measure of policyholder security than any internal measure. However, any regulatory requirement to undertake an ORSA may include a solvency capital projection which may be equivalent (or similar) to an internal economic capital assessment.
• Rating agency capital – Should understand the capital adequacy standards used by rating agencies as this can drive the cost of raising additional capital.
• Solvency projections can be complicated by the need for nested stochastic simulations, simplifications may be used, eg closed-form solutions and proxy models.

The relationship between capital, risk and economic value
• Risk, capital and value are inextricably linked: risk exposure drives capital needs; the market price of financial risk drives the shareholders’ required return; shareholder value is modified by the frictional costs of holding capital.

Methods of managing the capital position
• Methods that can raise additional available capital or reduce liabilities and/or required capital include: equity; subordinated loan stock; securitisation; reinsurance; derivatives – both static and dynamic strategies; asset-liability matching.
• Securitisation and financial reinsurance are less effective in regulatory regimes which take credit for future profits in the regulatory balance sheet, eg under Solvency II.

17.title: Uses and Benefits of Reinsurance
17.summary:
Reinsurance is used for the following reasons:
• to control risk: to limit exposure to risk; to enable larger risks to be written; to spread risks
• to smooth profits
• financial assistance: to help with new business strain; to help with solvency position; to help with takeovers
• technical assistance: new products, markets and techniques; access to data; sharing of risk; technical training; assessment of future trends; underwriting; claims control; product design; policy wording; overseas developments; IT software.

Reinsurance should be, but isn’t always, a self-balancing item.

Reinsurance can take advantage of tax or regulatory arbitrage.

Reinsurers can either charge insurers a fee for their services and products, or take a share of profitable business, or both.

Badging, where insurers make use of specialist insurers’ expertise, is an alternative to reinsurance.

18.title: Asset-Liability Management
18.summary:
Investment principles
• To maximise investment return, subject to meeting all contractual and TCF obligations and recognising the uncertainties involved and the overall risk that shareholders (of proprietary companies), regulators and policyholders will tolerate.

Asset-liability management
• Main aim is to determine a suitable investment strategy.
• Can investigate asset-liability matching requirements using deterministic or stochastic model office projections.

The effect of liabilities
• Assets chosen that are appropriate for the liabilities, ie assets should have similar term to the liabilities and respond to background influences (eg inflation and currency movements) in the same way that the liabilities do.
• The uncertainty underlying the timing of liability cashflows means that insurers need a high level of marketability for many of their assets.

ALM process
• Ease of matching depends on the type of business involved.
• Involves a liability projection system and an asset model.
• An economic scenario generator (ESG) is a model that produces numerous simulations of possible future economic outcomes, used as an input to the stochastic projection model.

Derivatives
• Used as an integral part of a company’s asset-liability strategies, typically to protect against a severe adverse scenario that only arises in limited circumstances.

The effect of free assets
• Large free assets allow a more risky investment policy, eg more volatile assets, fewer ‘secure assets’, mismatching by term, nature and currency.

Future investment income and capital values
• Asset proceeds projections performed separately for each asset class: fixed-interest and index-linked securities, equities and property. Cash assumed as an infinite income stream, an initial lump sum inflow or a combination.
• Consider the volatility in the capital values and income from assets and how volatility differs between asset types.
• Projections of assets and liabilities useful when the relationship between the two is looked at. Either a deterministic or a stochastic model could be used.
• Asset-liability models can incorporate an assessment of the likely level of solvency at each period in the future.

20.title: Analysis of Surplus, Embedded Value and Profit

subsections.title: Overview
bullets: This chapter follows from Chapter 19 (analysis of experience) and introduces three tools for analysing company performance: analysis of surplus (for long-term business such as CI), analysis of embedded value (for long-term business), and analysis of profit (for short-term business such as PMI).
The chapter also considers customer value as a measure of profitability.

subsections.title: Purpose and Definition
bullets: An analysis of surplus provides a reconciliation between the start-year and end-year supervisory surplus, breaking the change down into contributory factors.
Surplus is defined as assets minus liabilities. 'Surplus arising' over a period is the change in the amount of surplus during that time — this can be positive or negative.
The analysis is an attempt to quantify the various elements that have caused the total change in surplus. It is better described as an 'analysis of surplus arising'.

A complication is 'interaction terms' — for example, where expenses are lower than assumed and investment returns are higher. An element of surplus arises from earning excess return on assets that were not 'supposed' to be there; this could be attributed to either expense surplus or investment return surplus. There is no uniquely correct approach, so a systematic method (Section 3) is used to ensure interaction terms are neither missed nor double-counted.

Reasons to perform an analysis of surplus: show the financial effect of divergences between valuation assumptions and actual experience; determine which assumptions are most financially significant; show the financial effect of writing new business; validate calculations and assumptions; provide a check on valuation data and process; identify non-recurring components; reconcile values for successive years; provide management information; provide data for executive remuneration schemes; provide information for publication; demonstrate that the variance in individual sources is a complete description of the variance in the total; give information on trends for the actuarial control cycle; comply with regulatory requirements (e.g. under Solvency II, profit and loss attribution is an internal model approval requirement).

key_terms.definition: Assets minus liabilities under the applicable supervisory (regulatory) valuation basis.
An element of surplus that arises as a combined effect of two or more sources of variance, making it ambiguous which single source it should be attributed to.
A form of analysis of surplus required under Solvency II as part of the internal model approval process, attributing changes in financial position to their sources.

exam_qa.q: Why might an insurance company want to perform an analysis of surplus?
exam_qa.a: To show the financial effect of divergences between valuation assumptions and actual experience; determine the most financially significant assumptions; show the effect of new business; validate calculations and assumptions; provide a check on data and process; identify non-recurring components to inform distribution decisions; reconcile successive years' values; provide management information and executive remuneration data; provide publication information; demonstrate completeness of the variance description; give trend information for the actuarial control cycle; and comply with regulatory requirements such as Solvency II P&L attribution.

subsections.title: The Sources of Surplus
bullets: Surplus arises as a result of differences between what was expected to happen (per the valuation assumptions, including implicit assumptions such as no new business) and what actually happened. Any change in the valuation assumptions themselves will also give rise to positive or negative surplus.
Possible components of the analysis of a simplified supervisory surplus (assets minus liabilities) are as follows.

Opening adjustments usually reflect changes to the model or data used to calculate the surplus, including corrections, changes to model point groupings, and methodology changes (e.g. changes to economic scenario generators or proxy modelling approaches).

Return on opening surplus results from investment returns on the opening surplus. This may be expressed as the actual return, or as the expected (risk-free) return only, with any actual difference recorded in the next item. For example, a surplus of 200 at the start of the year with 0.5% risk-free return and 1.0% actual return gives an 'expected' return of 1 and a 'difference between actual and expected' of 11 (applied to total assets), or alternatively 2 of 'actual return on surplus' and 10 of 'excess return on BEL-backing assets'.

Economic variances result from differences between actual and expected investment returns, inflation, and currency exchange rates. These will usually be the major source of variation. They include mismatch profits or losses where assets backing liabilities do not move in line with the liabilities following changes in economic conditions. Changes in asset mix may be separately identified. Under Solvency II, where a matching or volatility adjustment is used, the surplus will reflect the difference between actual spread movements (and defaults) relative to those allowed for in the adjustments.

The actual-to-expected investment return item may be split between the actual return relative to an internal view of the expected return, and this expected return relative to the expected investment return (the valuation discount rate).

Changes in economic assumptions may be shown separately or combined with economic variances, due to the close relationship between actual economic outcomes and the expected economic assumptions, particularly regarding yields and bond value movements.

Non-economic (or insurance) variances result from differences between actual and expected demographic and operational factors such as morbidity, mortality, persistency, and expenses. Variances from one-off or exceptional expenses may be identified separately.
Changes to non-economic (or insurance) assumptions may be shown separately or combined with the insurance variances.
New business impacts on surplus if liabilities differ from the assets accrued in respect of new policies (premiums less initial outgo, plus investment return to valuation date).
Other variances might include capital injected into the fund or tax changes.

Unexplained movements arise because it is unrealistic to expect the entire movement in surplus to be fully explained. Management should specify an acceptable level of unexplained movement (e.g. as a percentage of total liabilities), but should be mindful that a small residual could mask material errors that broadly offset each other.

When deciding how to classify variances, consistency from one year to the next is important so that year-by-year comparisons remain meaningful. For example, changes in policyholder lapse rates driven by economic conditions could be classed as either non-economic or economic variance.

key_terms.definition: Surplus arising from differences between actual and expected investment returns, inflation, and currency exchange rates — typically the largest source of variance.
Surplus arising from differences between actual and expected demographic and operational factors such as morbidity, mortality, persistency, and expenses.
Adjustments made to the opening surplus to reflect corrections, methodology changes, or changes to model point groupings before the analysis begins.
The residual difference between the final rolled-forward balance sheet and the actual year-end balance sheet that cannot be attributed to any identified source.

Under Solvency II, an addition to the risk-free discount rate allowed for insurers holding qualifying illiquid assets, derived using EIOPA-published fundamental spreads. Differences between actual and assumed credit experience give rise to investment variances.

exam_qa.q: List the possible components of an analysis of supervisory valuation surplus.
exam_qa.a: Opening adjustments; return on opening surplus; economic variances (investment returns, inflation, currency); changes in economic assumptions; non-economic (insurance) variances (morbidity, mortality, persistency, expenses); changes in non-economic assumptions; new business impact; other variances (e.g. capital injections, tax changes); unexplained movements.

exam_qa.q: Why is consistency in the classification of variances important in an analysis of surplus?
exam_qa.a: Because the purpose of the analysis is partly to identify trends in experience. If the same item is classified differently from one year to the next — for example, lapse rate changes due to economic conditions being classified as non-economic in one year and economic in another — year-by-year comparisons become meaningless and misleading.

sections.title: Quantifying the Surplus Arising from Each Source

subsections.title: The Balance Sheet Projection Approach
bullets: For the analysis of surplus to act as a check that no errors have been made in the valuation, it is essential that a logical and robust calculation process is in place.

A typical process is to project forward the insurer's previous supervisory balance sheet assuming experience was as per the insurer's valuation basis. This involves rolling forward the balance sheet from the previous valuation date (both assets and liabilities) allowing in turn for corrections, assumption changes, changes to economic conditions, new business and experience variances, to produce an expected balance sheet. The actual and expected balance sheets can then be compared, the difference being the 'unexplained'.

The process is equivalent to re-running the valuation model one element at a time.
